At the heart of each EOS kernel lies the Process Control Block <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(P C B)$</tex>, the data structure that defines task identity, execution context, and inter-process coordination. Although scheduling algorithms and communication mechanisms in realtime operating systems (RTOSes) have been widely studied, the internal PCB design which directly governs memory footprint, context-switch latency, and energy efficiency has received comparatively little systematic analysis. This paper provides a comparative review of PCB architectures across representative EOS families. We examine their PCB field structures, memory costs, scheduling coupling, and protection features, revealing the trade-offs between simplicity and capability in different real-time environments. A five-class taxonomy is proposed, ranging from Stackless to Microkernel PCB models, highlighting the evolutionary trend from cooperative, single-threaded designs to SMP-aware and secure multi-domain kernels. Finally, we identify emerging research directions such as dynamic PCB allocation, energy-adaptive scheduling, and Memory Protection Unit (MPU)-based lightweight isolation. The review aims to provide both researchers and developers with a structural understanding of process management in EOSes, serving as a baseline for future design of ultra-lightweight yet deterministic kernel architectures.

title: Head Reconstruction Using 3D Keypoint Constraints
authors: Wanshu Feng, Gang Zhang, Mingjian Gao
publish: 2025 3rd International Conference on Signal Processing and Intelligent Computing (SPIC)
abstract: Head reconstruction remains a fundamental challenge in computer vision, especially when only limited geometric cues are available for guiding the recovery of finegrained facial structure. Considering the limitations of using 2D keypoints constraints, this paper proposes a head reconstruction method that introduces 3D keypoints as constraints. The FLAME parametric model is used as an auxiliary model, transforming the head reconstruction task into the estimation of model parameters. An encoder-decoder network is used to estimate the shape, pose, and expression parameters. During head mesh optimization, the 3D keypoints are used as constraints to guide the reconstruction process, and the loss function is designed to account for the difference between the ground-truth and predicted 3D keypoints. Experimental results demonstrate that, due to the use of 3D keypoint constraints, the proposed method obtains the alignment error 1.34 and is superior to methods using 2D keypoint constraints.

title: Predictive Modeling of Groundwater Depletion Using Ensemble Machine Learning and Geospatial Data
authors: K. SenthilVadivu, G.P. Berathsaran, R K. Kiruthikvarshan
publish: 2026 International Conference on Visual Analytics and Data Visualization (ICVADV)
abstract: Accelerated urbanization, climate change, and unsustainable water usage practices are making groundwater issues more serious. Conventional monitoring methods usually lack a predictive component, are less accurate, and take a lot of time. The suggested system predicts groundwater levels using machine learning techniques based on conductivity, pH, temperature, and other environmental and water quality factors. The model is trained on historical data using XGBoost, a highperformance implementation of the gradient boosting technique. A straightforward Stream lit web application is then used to host the trained machine learning model, providing a user-friendly interface for real-time prediction access. This prediction algorithm offers a quick and reliable way to manage water resources and issue early warnings. Additionally, the technology can assist decision-makers in developing conservation and sustainable water usage plans. Compared to manual monitoring, the predictive model will be more accurate and scalable. Additionally, the approach is affordable, necessitates little infrastructure, and makes long-term planning of water resources and use easier. All things considered, this initiative will make it possible to make informed forecasts that will support sustainable groundwater management.

title: Multivariate Spatial Modelling of Tree Species Distribution Using Adaptive Sparse Group Lasso: Case Study in Barro Colorado Island Plot
authors: Refki Assadiky, Achmad Choiruddin, Santi Puteri Rahayu
publish: 2025 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Blockchain, Cloud Computing, and Data Analytics (ICoABCD)
abstract: Multivariate spatial point patterns have gained significant popularity as technology enables large-scale data collection. This study focuses on modeling tree species distribution in the Barro Colorado Island (BCI) forest plot, considering 9 tree species and 13 environmental covariates. To understand species co-occurrence with their environment and each other, we apply the inhomogeneous multivariate Poisson point process (IMPP) model. The Random Quadrature (RQ) method is used for efficient parameter estimation, aligning with generalized linear models while minimizing redundant computations. Given the high density of covariates, we apply the adaptive sparse group lasso (ASGL) for variable selection, focusing on the most influential environmental variables per species. The model using ASGL shows superior performance, with an AIC value of 158849.6, indicating a better fit compared to the model without ASGL. The study identifies key environmental factors such as elevation, slope, potassium content, phosphorus content, and solar radiation that significantly influence species distribution. Finally, hierarchical clustering identifies three distinct habitat groups based on their estimated environmental responses. The clustering results reveal three distinct habitat groups. These findings offer valuable insights for afforestation and reforestation efforts.

title: Lightweight YOLO Architectures for Efficient Dog Emotion Classification
authors: Viencentius Delvin Frelim, Martin Sebastian, Auryn Larissa Sabrina, Samuel Philip
publish: 2025 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Blockchain, Cloud Computing, and Data Analytics (ICoABCD)
abstract: This study investigates the effectiveness of lightweight YOLO based classification architectures, namely YOLOv8s cls and YOLOv11s cls, for automatic dog emotion recognition. The emotions angry, happy, relaxed, and sad are defined according to the Circumplex Model of Affect. A curated dataset consisting of 3,600 images, with 900 images per class, was constructed and preprocessed through quality filtering, duplicate removal, and feature based clustering to ensure data diversity and class balance. To guarantee a fair comparison, both models were trained using identical preprocessing pipelines, image resolutions, learning rates, batch sizes, epoch numbers, and evaluation metrics. Model training and validation were conducted using five fold cross validation, while final performance was evaluated on a separate test set excluded from training and validation. Experimental results show that YOLOv8s cls achieved the best performance, with an accuracy of 86.42 percent and a macro average <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$F 1$</tex> score of 86.43 percent, surpassing YOLOv11s cls, which obtained 85.28 percent accuracy and an F1 score of 85.22 percent. Both YOLO based models also outperformed conventional convolutional neural network architectures such as VGG16 and ResNet50 reported previously. These findings highlight the potential of lightweight YOLO classifiers as fast, accurate, and resource efficient solutions for real time dog emotion recognition.

title:
Progress in the Detection of Mesosphere and Lower Thermosphere Temperatures Based on the O
                    <sub>2</sub>
                    A-Band Airglow
authors: Weijia Wang, Taoyuan Zhang, Yachong Zhou, Chuanyuan Zhou
publish: 2025 3rd International Conference on Signal Processing and Intelligent Computing (SPIC)
abstract: The <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{O}_{2}$</tex> A-band <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(762 \text{nm})$</tex> serves as an excellent target emission for atmospheric temperature detection in the mesosphere-low thermosphere (MLT) region. This paper reviews the observation records of the A-band and summarizes its production mechanisms. The principles of key interferometric spectrometers such as the Fabry-Perot interferometer, Michelson interferometer, and spatial heterodyne spectrometer for temperature measurements in the MLT region are discussed and compared. Additionally, the technological advancements in this field are summarized and future development prospects are analyzed. This research provides novel technological insights for the fields of hyperspectral and multispectral imaging by conducting an in-depth analysis of various advanced interferometric spectrometer techniques based on O<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf> A-band airglow. It will significantly enhance the accuracy and efficiency of atmospheric temperature detection and extends the application scope of multispectral detection.

title: Preface: Committees
authors: N/A
publish: 2025 3rd International Conference on Signal Processing and Intelligent Computing (SPIC)

title: Automatic Detection of Central Serous Retinopathy Using Faster R-CNN
authors: Thokala Nikhitha, Katkam Himabindu, Jatoth Rahul, Kachi Anvesh, E Ravi Kumar, Muni Sekhar Velpuru
publish: 2026 International Conference on Visual Analytics and Data Visualization (ICVADV)
abstract: Central Serous Retinopathy (CSR) refers to a retinal disorder marked by the presence of fluid beneath the retina, which, if not identified at the initial stages, can cause distorted vision and permanent vision loss. Inappropriate identification of the disease depends entirely upon the interpretation of the images, which are time-consuming. To deal with this issue, this study removes this limitation by applying an image recognition tool called the Faster Region-Based Convolutional Neural Network (Faster R-CNN) to detect the location of the disease. In this study, the method aims to detect lesions by determining the bounding box of the lesions, which are influenced by the Central Serous Retinopathy disease. To begin with, images from publicly accessible sources are pre-processed. In pre-processing, the images are passed through resizing, normalization, and techniques involving the adjustment of image contrasts. Bounding boxes, which are annotated with the help of professionals, are used to mark the regions to be identified. Using the Faster R-CNN method, the regions can be precisely identified because the ResNet50 algorithm assists the model to learn features. Performance assessment shows the method to be relevant, reporting that it has an accuracy of 89%, Mean Average Precision of 0.83, and an F1-score of 0.83, which shows the efficiency of the method.

title: Spatiotemporal Integrated Optimization of Intersection with Inner-Widened Short Lanes
authors: Mengru Liu
publish: 2025 3rd International Conference on Signal Processing and Intelligent Computing (SPIC)
abstract: Short lanes are commonly adopted when physical constraints render full lane expansion infeasible at intersections. This study proposes a spatio-temporal integrated intersection design model featuring inner widened short lanes - a design that converts exit lanes into entry lanes through strategic innermost lane widening. Each additional lane requiring 3.0-3.2 meters of width. The proposed model incorporates both number and allocation of short lanes as decision variables into the conventional lane-based model, overcoming the limitations of existing research confined to fixed short lane number and predetermined lane allocation. Accordingly, choosing intersection capacity as the objective, we formulate the design model as a binary mixed-integer nonlinear programming (BMINLP) problem that simultaneously determines the optimal cycle length, timing plan, and lane allocation. Results show that when short lane capacity reaches 10 vehicles, implementing inner widened short lanes increases intersection capacity by 21% compared to conventional designs. When capacity exceeds the threshold of 4 vehicles, the optimal cycle length no longer adopts the maximum value and shows positive correlation with storage capacity. These findings provide theoretical basis for intersection design optimization and establish practical benchmarks for validating data-driven control strategies in intelligent transportation systems.

title: Smart Contract Implementation in Web-Based E-Voting Systems: Blockchain Security and Transparency Analysis
authors: Bryan Liem, Vincent Nicholas Hie, Zulfany Erlisa Rasjid
publish: 2025 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Blockchain, Cloud Computing, and Data Analytics (ICoABCD)
abstract: Traditional e-voting systems face critical challenges that undermine their security, transparency, and integrity, including data manipulation and vote fraud. This study addresses these issues by proposing a novel, blockchain based solution designed to enforce election rules securely without centralized control. We design and implement a web based e-voting prototype using smart contracts on the Ethereum blockchain, leveraging a decentralized stack that integrates React, MetaMask, Ethers.js, and Hardhat. While previous research has explored blockchain for e-voting, many studies focus on architecture but lack a comprehensive security analysis of the underlying smart contracts. Our key contribution lies in a rigorous, multi-faceted security analysis that validates the system's robustness. We employed static analysis with Slither, which successfully identified and mitigated specific timestamp dependency vulnerabilities, ensuring code quality. Furthermore, we achieved a 100% statement, function, and line coverage through unit testing by executing 25 test cases in under one second, and conducted extensive fuzz testing with Echidna, which reported no invariant violations. The results confirm the system's ability to ensure voter anonymity through cryptographic hashing, prevent double-voting, and maintain the immutability of the ballot ledger, directly addressing the vulnerabilities found in conventional systems. This research not only provides a practical, verifiable solution for secure e-voting but also establishes a robust methodology for auditing the security of smart contracts in high-stakes applications.

title: Access Algorithm of Heterogeneous Wireless Network Based on CLPSO Fuzzy Neural Network
authors: Xin Chang, Zhengang Shi, Yuantong Li
publish: 2025 3rd International Conference on Signal Processing and Intelligent Computing (SPIC)
abstract: In order to improve the access performance of heterogeneous wireless networks, this paper proposes an access selection based on CLPSO (Competitive Learning Particle Swarm Optimization) fuzzy neural network algorithm, which uses a competitive learning method to solve the problem of slow convergence speed and easy to fall into local excellence of traditional PSO(particle swarm optimization) fuzzy neural networks, adopts partition processing for particle swarms, Regarding the particle division process of the CLPSO algorithm, first, the fitness values of all particles in the population are calculated and sorted in ascending order. Then, the mean and standard deviation are computed based on these fitness values, and at the same time, the particle whose fitness value is closest to the average fitness value is marked. Subsequently, a range is defined with the standard deviation as the radius and selects the access of heterogeneous wireless networks by using partition processing for particle swarms and uses the optimal location found as the initial value of the membership function of the second and fifth layers of fuzzy neural networks. Finally, the proposed algorithm is compared with the Fuzzy-Logic algorithm, FNN algorithm and PSO fuzzy neural network algorithm (PSOFNN), The simulation results show that in networks with different numbers of users, the variations in access block rate and packet block rate are not significant. When the number of users reaches 30, the differences between these three indicators are not obvious, but when the number of users increases to 40, this difference becomes more noticeable. And when the number of users reaches 80, the block rate of CLPSO decreases even more significantly[11] and the results show that the proposed algorithm reduces the network access blockage rate, increases the system throughput, and improves the service quality of the access judgment on the basis of ensuring the Qos of the service users.

title: Ghost-YOLOv8-S: A Lightweight Network Design for Real-Time Traffic Light Detection
authors: Yuelin Tong, Yong Dai
publish: 2025 3rd International Conference on Signal Processing and Intelligent Computing (SPIC)
abstract: With the development of autonomous driving technology, traffic light detection has become a core part of vehicle environment perception. However, existing detection models often face high computational costs and large numbers of parameters when deployed on embedded devices. To address this issue, this paper proposes a lightweight improved model called Ghost-YOLOv8-S and optimizes it in the following two ways. First, the standard convolution layers in YOLOv8's backbone are replaced with lightweight GhostNet modules. These modules generate feature maps using low-cost linear operations, which significantly reduce the model's complexity and number of parameters. Second, based on the knowledge that traffic lights are usually small or medium-sized objects, the model removes the detection scale for large objects and keeps the scales for small and medium objects. This adjustment makes the network structure more suitable for the task. Experiments on a self-collected traffic light dataset show that Ghost-YOLOv8-S reduces model size by 41.2% and increases inference speed by 18.3% compared to the baseline YOLOv8n model, achieving a good balance between accuracy and efficiency. This study provides an effective solution for deploying perception systems on edge devices in autonomous vehicles and has broad application potential.

title: Research on Confirmation Mechanisms in Spatial Links
authors: Yushuo Liu, Lishi Liu
publish: 2025 3rd International Conference on Signal Processing and Intelligent Computing (SPIC)
abstract: In space links, due to sudden drops in communication quality on the forward link channel, reverse link congestion and low forward link throughput occur. Therefore, traditional transmission control protocols (TCP) cannot meet the communication requirements of space links. To address this issue, this paper focuses on the acknowledgment mechanism and proposes the TACK-N(TACK-NEW) mechanism based on the concept of the TACK(Tamed Acknowledgment) mechanism. The aim is to improve the shortcomings of the TACK mechanism by adjusting the frequency and quantity of returned acknowledgment information. This enhances the reverse link's congestion resistance and increases the forward link's throughput. Simulations conducted on the OMNET++ platform demonstrate that the TACK-N mechanism achieves an average throughput improvement of approximately 155% compared to the traditional TACK mechanism under high bit error rate scenarios, effectively alleviating reverse link congestion. This research provides a novel technical approach for efficient and reliable transmission over space links, holding significant application value for deep-space communications and satellite networks.

title: Research on Cross-Domain Gesture Recognition Method Based on Prototype Networks
authors: Chong Guo, Chenghao Wu, Hequn Li
publish: 2025 3rd International Conference on Signal Processing and Intelligent Computing (SPIC)
abstract: To address the insufficient generalization in cross-domain gesture recognition caused by domain shift and sparse target domain samples, this paper proposes a method combining ResNet18 with CBAM and an improved prototype network. CBAM is introduced in residual blocks to suppress redundancy and enhance gesture-relevant salient regions. The prototype network employs a joint loss function of cross-entropy and triplet contrast, narrowing similar classes and widening dissimilar classes distances in the prototype embedding space to enhance metric discriminability. The synergistic optimization simultaneously enhances domain-invariant representation and metric space separability. Cross-domain experiments based on Widar 3.0 demonstrate that our method achieves an average recognition rate of 88.7% across four cross-domain tasks. Compared to WiGr and EI, the average accuracy improved by approximately 5.5% and 7.0%, respectively. Compared to Widar, the average accuracy is slightly higher, and our method demonstrates significant advantages in cross-user and cross-direction tasks. Experimental results demonstrate that this method maintains excellent cross-domain generalization and recognition efficiency even with limited samples.

title: Review of Person Identification Techniques Using Bronchial Sound
authors: Anagha Ramnath Kadve, Grantej V. Otari
publish: 2026 International Conference on Visual Analytics and Data Visualization (ICVADV)
abstract: The concept of sound-based person identification (SPID) refers to a process of identifying or recognizing an individual's identity through their bronchial sounds, footsteps, human stride, as well as breathing sounds. For sound-based identification, pattern recognition, and acoustic signature analysis remain core technologies, where these technologies effectively conform the individual identity and are utilized in several domains such as surveillance, banking, healthcare, and law enforcement. Besides, various approaches have been installed recently to recognize the person based on human-sounding activities, in which the conventional models are labour-intensive, which maximizes the computational cost, and the variability associated with human labour. The study examined about 25 research papers for effective recognition in order to obtain a better understanding of bronchial sound-based PID. Eventually, the main objective of this research is to find the research gaps in the conventional models and aim for an effective bronchial sound-based PID in the future.

title: SDFUECR: Software-Defined and Fuzzy-Based Unequal Clustering Energy-Efficient Cluster Routing
authors: Thi Thuy Dung Tran, Ying Liang
publish: 2025 3rd International Conference on Signal Processing and Intelligent Computing (SPIC)
abstract: Wireless Sensor Networks (WSNs) face energy imbalance due to the “hotspot problem,” where cluster heads near the sink drain energy faster. This paper aims to solve this by proposing a new protocol named SDFUECR (Software-Defined Fuzzy Unequal Energy-efficient Cluster Routing), to mitigate this issue. Traditional routing protocols impose high computational loads on sensor nodes and result in uneven energy distribution. SDFUECR addresses these shortcomings through a synergistic integration of Software-Defined Networking (SDN) and fuzzy logic. The SDN controller provides centralized, network-wide orchestration for global optimization, while the fuzzy logic system executes adaptive cluster head selection and unequal clustering based on local node parameters like residual energy, distance to the sink, and node density. Simulation results demonstrate that SDFUECR significantly outperforms existing protocols, achieving network lifetime improvements of 54.5%, 46.4%, and 9.9% compared to the Fuzzy-Based Unequal Clustering Algorithm (FUCA), Hybrid Unequal Energy-Efficient Clustering (HEEC), and Fuzzy-based Multi-hop Unequal Cluster Routing (FMUCR) schemes, respectively. It also achieves more balanced energy consumption and reduced control overhead.

title: Development and Testing of an RFID-Based Library Circulation System Using ESP32 and MySQL
authors: Adi Wijaya, Resa Pramudita, Andre Reyvaldo Iglesias, Ibnu Hartopo, Jaja Kustija
publish: 2025 5th International Symposium on Materials and Electrical Engineering (ISMEE)
abstract: This study introduces the development and evaluation of an RFID-based library circulation system using the ESP32 microcontroller and a MySQL database to optimize book lending and returning processes. Conventional library systems often face challenges, such as time-consuming manual data entry, limited accuracy, and inefficiencies in managing book inventories. To address these issues, the proposed system integrates Radio Frequency Identification (RFID) technology for automatic book and user identification, coupled with a web-based database for real-time monitoring and record management. The hardware prototype was built using ESP32, RFID readers, LCD displays, and supporting components, while the software was implemented using Arduino IDE, PHP, and MySQL. Testing included functional verification, RFID performance measurements, system integration testing (SIT), and user acceptance testing (UAT). Results demonstrated that all hardware and software components performed as intended, with RFID successfully reading various tags within practical distances. System integration allowed seamless data transfer between hardware and database, while UAT confirmed usability and reliability from the user perspective. The findings highlight that RFID-based systems can significantly reduce staff workload, minimize errors, and provide faster, user-friendly self-service options in library operations. This research contributes to modernizing library infrastructure and demonstrates the practical benefits of combining microcontroller technology with database management.

title: Real-Time and Energy-Efficient VLC Indoor Localization Using Dimming-Aware Modulation and Lightweight Machine Learning
authors: Edo Lutfi Mahanani, Aminah Indahsari Marsuki, M. Imansyah Basudewa
publish: 2025 5th International Symposium on Materials and Electrical Engineering (ISMEE)
abstract: This paper presents a real-time and energy-efficient indoor localization system that leverages Visible Light Communication (VLC) for both data transmission and positioning. To address the challenges of illumination control and accurate localization, the proposed method integrates a dimming-aware Variable Pulse Position Modulation (VPPM) scheme with a lightweight Random Forest (RF) machine learning model. The system is evaluated using a dataset of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{5, 6 2 5}$</tex> samples with 20 features, providing a solid basis for classification and regression tasks. Experimental results show that the proposed framework achieves a zone classification accuracy of 97.93% and mean localization error of 1.20 m. Beyond classification, regression-based estimation significantly enhances precision, reducing the mean error to 0.0835 m and the median error to 0.0669 m. Real-time simulations further confirm the stability of the approach, with an overall mean error of 0.214 m and a final accuracy of 98%, demonstrating reliable performance under practical conditions. In addition to its accuracy, the system exhibits extremely low energy consumption, requiring only <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$2.0 \times 10^{-11} \mathrm{J}$</tex> per symbol and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$2.0 \times 10^{-10} \mathrm{J}$</tex> per localization for <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$M=10$</tex>. These results highlight the potential of combining dimming-aware modulation with machine learning to achieve practical, energy-efficient, and high-accuracy indoor localization, making the proposed system a strong candidate for next-generation VLC-based smart environments.

title: Enhancing AI Chatbot Responsiveness Through Design of a Multimodal Emotion Detection
authors: Yashraj Sah, Atharva Padole, Mahesh Dongre, Gitanjali Shinde, Grishma Bobhate, Sonal Fatangare
publish: 2026 International Conference on Visual Analytics and Data Visualization (ICVADV)
abstract: The research objective focuses on building emotion-based chatbots using artificial intelligence. Text-based bots fail to detect emotions which makes them incapable of producing suitable responses. Wav2Vec 2.0 NLP and Vision Transformer analysis enable the system to identify voice tones which solves this issue. The system utilizes real-time observation for monitoring many interviewees at once and measuring classroom engagement of students while identifying AI-generated tests. This research combines multimodal attention functions with reinforcement learning algorithms to control cultural and unknown user preferences and provides limited ethical protection during execution. This chatbot performs emotional detection at a human standard while adhering to ethical guidelines in AI practice. Complex systems require integrated systems of deep learning and neuro-symbolic artificial intelligence to attain ethical protection characteristics during their decision-making processes.

title: A Novel AI-Powered Ensemble Model for Effective Malware Classification in Android Security
authors: Hussana Johar R B, Manimala S, Bhavya H, Uzma Tabassum, Gayathri S
publish: 2025 International Conference on Emerging Technologies in Electronics and Green Energy (ICETEG)
abstract: There is a growing need for effective detection systems due to the rise in malware threats caused by the widespread use of Android-based devices. An efficient malware detection system must be put into place without delay in light of this predicament. A hybrid deep learning model combining Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) and Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) is presented in this study as a new way to classify Android malware. To train and assess the efficacy of the suggested model, the experiment makes use of the CICInvesAndMal2019 dataset, which has 10,854 samples. Exhibiting its resilience in identifying both harmless and harmful Android apps, the model accomplishes remarkable performance with an accuracy of 99.16%, precision of 99.98%, recall of 99.87%, and F1score of 99.76%. The model outperforms more conventional machine learning techniques by a wide margin, including Decision Tree (91.64%), Random Forest (97.16%), and Support Vector Machine (94.36%). This finding provides important evidence that deep learning approaches have great promise for enhancing Android malware classification and providing a solid answer for cybersecurity uses. The suggested model is praised for its exceptional accuracy and dependability in detecting Android malware, as shown in this paper's thorough assessment of the model.

title: Bluetooth Controlled Robot with Real-Time Obstacle Detection and Live Video Feedback
authors: Sumukh A V, Nagarathna S, Nanda S
publish: 2025 International Conference on Emerging Technologies in Electronics and Green Energy (ICETEG)
abstract: This paper presents the design and development of a cost-effective, Bluetooth-controlled mobile robot intended for industrial inspection and surveillance tasks, which integrates real-time obstacle detection and live video feedback. The system is built around an Arduino Mega 2560 microcontroller, which manages a servo-mounted HC-SR04 ultrasonic sensor and an ESP32-CAM module for live video streaming. Unlike conventional teleoperated robots that rely on a single data stream for situational awareness, this platform incorporates a systematic 180° ultrasonic scanning mechanism to generate a proximity map, which significantly enhances obstacle detection reliability and complements visual data. Experimental evaluation demonstrates a 95% obstacle detection accuracy within a 40 cm range, a stable video stream at 15 frames per second (FPS), and a system latency of 200ms. The Bluetooth connection proved robust up to 10 meters indoors. The system's modular, cost-effective design (under 150 $) facilitates enhanced operator perception in hazardous environments where fully autonomous solutions are often impractical. Compared to existing designs, this approach offers superior multi-sensor fusion capabilities in a low-cost package, which improves navigation safety during teleoperation.

title: Experimental Study of QoS Parameters in CasaOS and Nextcloud on Ubuntu 22.04
authors: Hane Yorda Dinata, Galura Muhammad Suranegara, Ahmad Fauzi
publish: 2025 5th International Symposium on Materials and Electrical Engineering (ISMEE)
abstract: The exponential growth of digital data has increased the demand for efficient and reliable cloud-storage solutions. This study presents a comparative experimental evaluation of two open-source platforms, CasaOS and Nextcloud, deployed on Ubuntu 22.04. The primary objective is to assess Quality of Service (QoS) performance measured by throughput, latency, jitter, and packet loss. Experiments involved file transfers of 1 MB, 10 MB, 100 MB, 500 MB, and 1000 MB executed under controlled virtualized conditions; each scenario was repeated five times and the reported values are averages. Network traffic was captured and analyzed with Wireshark. Results indicate that CasaOS provides more stable throughput (2.4-4.0 Mbps) with negligible packet loss (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\leq \text{0. 6 \%}$</tex>), albeit with higher latency and jitter (up to 24 ms and 25 ms). Nextcloud exhibits lower and more consistent latency and jitter (7-14 ms) while showing more variable throughput; importantly, no packet loss (0%) was observed across all Nextcloud scenarios. These findings suggest CasaOS is better suited for reliable large-file transfers, whereas Nextcloud is preferable for latency-sensitive applications. The study offers empirical guidance for selecting open-source cloud-storage platforms according to operational priorities.

title: MelSpectrogram-Based Voice Disorder Classification Using Bayesian-Optimised Convolutional Neural Networks
authors: M. Kalaiselvi, T. Jayasree
publish: 2026 International Conference on Visual Analytics and Data Visualization (ICVADV)
abstract: The prevalence of voice disorders is increasing annually, highlighting the need for accurate and cost-effective diagnostic tools. In this work, we developed an automated framework for detecting and classifying structural voice disorders, including papilloma, fibroma, cysts, and polyps. Voice samples were gathered from the Saarbrucken Voice Database (SVD) and the Voice Database. To precisely capture the time-frequency characteristics of diseased voices, each audio sample was converted into a Mel-spectrogram representation. The four disorders were then classified using a Bayesianoptimised Convolutional Neural Network (CNN). The suggested solution outperformed current methods in terms of performance and computational efficiency, achieving 96% accuracy on the publicly accessible SVD dataset. By enabling timely and economical remote evaluation, the method provides a practical tool for preliminary clinical screening of vocal disorders.

title: A Comparative Study of YOLOv8, Faster R-CNN, and SSD for Real-Time Object Detection
authors: Dewi Rahmawati, Fidi Wincoko Putro, Arni Muarifah, Devit Erlingga Arafiudin, Mochammad Ricky Pratama, Diandra Nabil Putra Cahyono
publish: 2026 International Seminar on Intelligent Business and Edge-Computing Research (ISIBER)
abstract: Visually impaired individuals face significant challenges in navigating safely, especially when encountering hazardous objects. This paper presents AI TunaNetra, a realtime sound- and haptic-based object detection system that classifies environmental threats into three categories: (1) moving obstacles (e.g., vehicles), (2) static obstacles (e.g., stairs), and (3) dangerous objects (e.g., broken glass, needles). Using a custom dataset of 5,800 annotated images from SLB Tunas Mulya Surabaya, we fine-tuned YOLOv8, Faster R-CNN, and SSD under identical protocols. Evaluated on a mid-range Android device (X, YOLOv8n achieved 94.1% mAP@0.5 and 48 MAP, with 89.6 % small-object recall significantly outperforming SSD300 (68.2 %) and Faster R-CNN (18 MAP). Distance to hazards is estimated via monocular scaling calibrated per-device <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(\pm 12 ~\text{cm}$</tex> error at <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$<1 ~\mathrm{m})$</tex>. The application delivers context-aware audio alerts (e.g., “STOP! Broken glass 0.5 meters ahead”) and haptic feedback for deafblind users. In a 4-week trial, collisions decreased by 90 %, self-reported confidence increased by 80 %, and guide reliance dropped by 60 %. The system operates and supports multimodal feedback for deafblind users.

title: Power Factor Correction in Low Voltage Distribution System Using Capacitor Bank in Educational Facilities
authors: Devinda Rizky Putri Azroi, Abdul Syakur, Susatyo Handoko
publish: 2025 5th International Symposium on Materials and Electrical Engineering (ISMEE)
abstract: The Power factor in low voltage distribution systems has declined due to the increasing use of inductive devices such as single-phase motors, fluorescent lamps, welding machines, and grinders. A low power factor leads to higher current flow, greater energy losses, and penalties for excessive reactive power demand. This study investigates the application of a capacitor bank in a single-phase low voltage network at Vocational School Islam Sudirman Ungaran to improve power factor and overall efficiency. Field measurements at a critical load point showed a power factor of 0.75, below the recommended threshold of 0.90. The system was modeled and simulated using Electrical Transient and Analysis Program (ETAP) version 19.0.1 to evaluate electrical performance before and after capacitor bank installation. The correction method involved injecting leading reactive power to counteract the lagging effect of inductive loads. Using optimal capacitor placement, a 100 kVAR bank was installed at Bus 3, supplying 97.4 kVAR and reducing the reactive power burden by 96.8%. This increased the power factor to 0.95 and reduced line current by 20.1%. These results confirm that targeted capacitor bank placement is a reliable and cost-effective strategy to enhance power quality, minimize technical losses, and ensure voltage stability in educational electrical systems.

title: Small Models, Significant Risks: Jailbreaking Attacks on Lightweight LLMs
authors: Mostafa Osama, Ali Hashish, Hla Hany, Omar Amin, Youssef Ghatas
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer, Data Sciences and Applications (ACDSA)
abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly integrated into real-world applications, including chatbots, AI assistants, and healthcare support systems. However, jailbreaking attacks - techniques that bypass safety constraints and induce harmful content - pose a critical threat to their safe deployment. These attacks are particularly concerning because they can be easily carried out through prompt engineering alone, without access to the models' internals. In this study, we evaluate four template-based jailbreak methods: Template-Jailbreak, Parameters, DeepInception, and Fallacy Failure, targeting widely-used lightweight LLMs: Gemma-3n, Phi-2, DeepSeek-R1, Llama-3.2, and Qwen3. For evaluation, we use a publicly available dataset of 60 malicious prompts, aligned with the malicious content category defined in OpenAI's policy compliance guidelines, and a finetuned RoBERTa model to automatically detect jailbreak success. Our results demonstrate that template-based attacks remain highly effective, revealing distinct trade-offs between attack efficiency and generalization. Overall, despite lightweight LLMs being generally vulnerable to these attacks, Gemma showed substantial improvements in robustness, although it remains vulnerable under certain conditions. These findings underscore the urgency of developing stronger defenses, as even state-of-the-art lightweight LLMs remain highly vulnerable to easily exploitable prompt-based jailbreak attacks.

title: Usage of Hashed Time-Frequency Constellation Analysis in Real-Time Network Traffic Fingerprinting for Anomaly Detection
authors: M. Uğur Öney, M. Mert Güler, O. Orçun Ada
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer, Data Sciences and Applications (ACDSA)
abstract: The real-time identification and classification of network traffic is a fundamental challenge in network management and cybersecurity. Traditional methods like Deep Packet Inspection (DPI) are often resource-intensive and struggle with encrypted traffic, while signature-based systems can be evaded by novel threats. This paper presents a novel, lightweight system for network traffic fingerprinting inspired by the principles of audio fingerprinting systems [1]. By transforming statistical features of network flow into a time-series signal, applying frequency analysis to identify dominant patterns, and generating robust hashes, the system can effectively identify and classify recurring traffic patterns in real time. This approach enables the rapid detection of known behaviors and the flagging of new, potentially anomalous activities, offering a powerful tool for network monitoring and cybersecurity applications.

title: EV Charging Scheduling Optimization Using PSO Algorithm for Cost Efficiency and Network Load Management
authors: Saiful Karim, Syafaruddin, Ansar Suyuti, Anuar Bin Mohamad
publish: 2025 5th International Symposium on Materials and Electrical Engineering (ISMEE)
abstract: The rapid rise of electric vehicles (EVs) is transforming the transportation sector, driving innovation and sustainability efforts worldwide. However, this growth also places considerable pressure on existing power systems, particularly during periods of high demand. A major challenge lies in managing the surge in peak electricity demand and the resulting increase in charging costs, which can strain both utilities and consumers. To address these challenges, effective scheduling strategies are required to balance grid stability while ensuring efficient and cost-effective energy utilization. In this study, Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) is employed as a computational method to optimize EV charging schedules. The approach focuses on identifying cost-saving patterns and improving charging behavior by distributing the charging load more evenly across time. The proposed model is designed to lower operational and charging expenses. Simulation results indicate that applying PSO can reduce costs by approximately <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$11-18 \%$</tex> compared to conventional scheduling methods, while also improving energy efficiency and enhancing overall load distribution. These outcomes highlight the potential of PSO as a practical and effective tool for supporting the sustainable integration of EVs into modern power systems and promoting a more reliable, resilient, and economically efficient energy infrastructure.

title: Time-Frequency Domain Based Glaucoma Detection Using Electroretinography
authors: Jenefa A V, Jemsina Banu S, Gogila Devi K
publish: 2026 International Conference on Visual Analytics and Data Visualization (ICVADV)
abstract: Glaucoma is a chronic eye disorder characterized by the gradual damage of retinal ganglion cells, which most often leads to irreversible vision loss if not detected at an early stage. The conventional diagnostic techniques, namely tonometry and visual field analysis, have their own setbacks due to the subjective nature and late-stage detection capability. In this context, this work proposes a Convolutional Neural Network-based framework for automated glaucoma detection using Electroretinogram signals. These ERG signals are processed by noise filtration, baseline correction, and signal normalization to improve signal quality. The preprocessed signals are then transformed into two-dimensional spectrograms by Time-Frequency Transformation, which allows the CNN to learn both time and frequency-based variations related to glaucomatous changes. These complex features are learned autonomously by the model to classify patients as normal or glaucomatous with high precision. Experimental analysis shows that the proposed CNN-based algorithm outperforms the conventional machine learning models in terms of accuracy and robustness. The approach offers a reliable, objective, and non-invasive diagnostic tool for early detection and better clinical management of glaucoma.

title: Development of an SRAM Neutron Detector for Pulsed Fusion Experiments
authors: Wolfgang Hennig, Nate Kaneshige, Sara Alawabdeh, Dan Cech, Michael Hua
publish: IEEE Transactions on Plasma Science
abstract: Radiation is known to cause single-event upsets in semiconductor devices, which leads to data errors in SRAM memory (bitflips). While usually undesirable, this effect can also be used as a mechanism to detect radiation, in particular neutrons. Previous research shows that SRAM memory is capable of detecting neutrons without permanent damage to the device, and that devices from different processes and/or manufacturers vary in properties such as neutron interaction energy threshold and overall cross section. In the work described here, an SRAM bit flip neutron detector was assembled, programmed, and tested with a variety of sources. Two SRAM types with identical chip package and functionality, from Renesas and Integrated Silicon Solution Inc. (ISSI), were tested with a Cf-252 source, deuterium-deuterium (DD) and deuterium-tritium (DT) neutron generators, and high-yield neutron pulses from the Godiva (fission) and Z-pinch experimental underground system (ZEUS) (DT fusion) facilities. Additional tests were performed with decapsulated chips and an alpha source. We observe that the number of bitflips increases linearly with the neutron fluence. The ISSI SRAM is physically a smaller die, but shows <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$2\times $</tex-math> </inline-formula>–<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$100\times $</tex-math> </inline-formula> more bit flips per neutron than the Renesas SRAM, depending on the neutron source. The bitflips are distributed randomly across memory addresses, except for groups of three close neighbors observed in the Renesas SRAM. No damage to the SRAM has been observed, even in the intense EMI and high instantaneous flux of the ZEUS pulsed neutron fields. With a suitable calibration for bitflips per incident neutrons, the detector can be used for quantitative measurements, and there is potential to use the ISSI versus Renesas type to differentiate between DT and DD neutrons. With a cost of <U.S. 10 per memory chip with four million bits, the design is highly scalable into large arrays for higher sensitivity. The chips are also conducive to rapid replacement in the case of operational damage. On Helion Energy’s Polaris fusion generator, the SRAM detector will be fielded as a relative yield neutron fluence detector with regular calibrations against indium and zirconium neutron activation foils.

title: An Enhanced Multiple Intrusion Event Localization Algorithm Based on Dual MZ-Sagnac Optical Fiber Sensing Perimeter Security System
authors: Yang Yan, Churui Li, Rui Jin, Chenyue He, Yuzhe Sun, Yunke Du, Chao Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Interferometric distributed optical fiber vibration sensing technology has been widely applied in the field of perimeter security. Among various structures, the dual MZ–Sagnac optical fiber sensing perimeter security system (the dual MZ–Sagnac system) plays an important role in perimeter security due to its high sensitivity, electromagnetic resistance, corrosion resistance, high stability and low cost. However, under the condition of low-SNR signals, the traditional event localization method for the dual MZ–Sagnac system, which directly averages the localization results of continuous short-interval signals, leads to great errors. In this paper, by employing the Cram´er-Rao Lower Bound (CRLB) theory of cross-correlation time delay estimation method, a Weighted Gaussian Superposition (WGS) algorithm based on the dual MZ–Sagnac system is proposed to enhance event localization accuracy and distinguish multiple vibration events. Vibration signals are collected and CRLB conformity to the dual MZ–Sagnac system is validated. Furthermore, the WGS algorithm reveals a minimum MSE of 1.63, corresponding to the error of 1.28 m and an accuracy improvement of 72 % in the experiment. Additionally, an application is performed to evaluate the effectiveness of the WGS algorithm for multiple events discrimination and localization. In perimeter security scenarios utilizing the dual MZ-Sagnac system, the WGS algorithm not only further refines the localization precision but also enables the system with multiple events discrimination, allowing the measurement instrumentation to better adapt to engineering applications.

title: Improving Out-of-Distribution Generalization on Point Clouds with Cross-Domain Adversarial Distillation
authors: Bowei Xing, Xianghua Ying, Ruibin Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Multimedia
abstract: Point cloud is an important data format to represent 3D objects and real world scenes, which is widely used in many fields such as virtual reality and autonomous driving. In recent years, various methods have been proposed for point cloud recognition and achieved great performance. However, since these methods are always under the i.i.d. assumption, the trained model inevitably suffers drastic performance degradation when evaluated on out-of-distribution (OOD) data. To address the above problem, in this paper, we propose a framework named cross-domain adversarial distillation, with the goal of learning a model that generalizes well on OOD point clouds. Concretely, we initiate by dynamically generating adversarial point cloud examples that deviate from original domain and exhibit diversification, so as to progressively broaden the training distribution. Subsequently, we implement a bidirectional distillation scheme to ensure semantic consistency between the generated novel-domain example and source domain example, which facilitates the exchange of cross-domain knowledge through teacher-student cooperation. By iteratively alternating between the above processes, the model is encouraged to learn more generalizable representations when encountering point clouds from novel distributions, leading to improved performance on the unseen target domain. We carry experiments on two typical datasets, ModelNet40-C for corrupted point clouds and Sim-to-Real for real-world point clouds, implemented with different 3D recognition models. The experimental results show that our method achieves better performance on these two benchmarks, which demonstrate the effectiveness of our method for improving point cloud OOD generalization.

title: Vulnerabilities in AI-Generated Image Detection: The Challenge of Adversarial Attacks
authors: Yunfeng Diao, Naixin Zhai, Changtao Miao, Zitong Yu, Xingxing Wei, Xun Yang, Meng Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Multimedia
abstract: Recent advancements in image synthesis, particularly with the advent of GAN and Diffusion models, have amplified public concerns regarding the dissemination of disinformation. To address such concerns, numerous AI-generated Image (AIGI) Detectors have been proposed and achieved promising performance in identifying fake images. However, there still lacks a systematic understanding of the adversarial robustness of AIGI detectors. In this paper, we examine the vulnerability of state-of-the-art AIGI detectors against adversarial attack under white-box and black-box settings, which has been rarely investigated so far. To this end, we propose a new method to attack AIGI detectors. First, inspired by the obvious difference between real images and fake images in the frequency domain, we add perturbations under the frequency domain to push the image away from its original frequency distribution. Second, we explore the full posterior distribution of the surrogate model to further narrow this gap between heterogeneous AIGI detectors, e.g., transferring adversarial examples across CNNs and ViTs. This is achieved by introducing a novel post-train Bayesian strategy that turns a single surrogate into a Bayesian one, capable of simulating diverse victim models using one pre-trained surrogate, without the need for re-training. We name our method as Frequency-based Post-train Bayesian Attack, or FPBA. Through FPBA, we demonstrate that adversarial attacks pose a real threat to AIGI detectors. FPBA can deliver successful black-box attacks across various detectors, generators, defense methods, and even evade cross-generator and compressed image detection, which are crucial real-world detection scenarios. Our code is available at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/onotoa/fpba</uri>.

title: GeneralizGeneralizing Beyond Patterns: Dynamic Moment Query Recalibrating for Out-of-Distribution Video Temporal Localizationing
authors: Zhuo Tao, Liang Li, Yunbin Tu, Jiong Yin, Beichen Zhang, Zheng-Jun Zha, Qingming Huang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Multimedia
abstract: Video temporal localization (VTL) aims to accurately identify the temporal boundary of a specific segment corresponding to a sentence query from an untrimmed video. Recently, DETR-based methods have emerged as effective solutions to VTL. The primary idea of these methods is to fuse multi-modal features and refine moment queries to obtain precise locations. Despite their success, these methods adopt fixed trainable moment queries, which implicitly model the statistical patterns of groundtruth segments in the training set and thus limit the model's generalization ability to out-of-distribution scenarios. To tackle this problem, we propose a Dynamic moment Query Recalibrated DEtection TRansformer (DQR-DETR) that adaptively generates and recalibrates multi-scale moment queries on the target segments for out-of-distribution VTL. Our method contains three key parts: (1) progressive semantic regularization that performs primitive modality consensus loss and refined semantic calibration loss at two phases for better global and fine-grained cross-modal associations; (2) hierarchical query decoupling that first dynamically extracts multi-scale and context-aware event queries from the joint visual-language representations to cover a wide range of potential events in video, and then select the sentence-aware queries with semantic guidance; (3) moment query recalibrating that performs coarse-to-fine modulation on multi-scale queries by first activating relevant queries with video highlight and sentence semantic guidance, and then refining them to focus on the target segments. Extensive experiments on QVHighlights, Charades-STA and TACoS datasets show that our DQR-DETR achieves state-of-the-art performance, especially in out-of-distribution scenarios where our method substantially outperforms existing approaches by large margins.

title: Dual Representation Aggregation Network for Blind Image Super-Resolution via Iterative Bi-level Optimization
authors: Xianhong Wen, Sheng Ren, Bin Hu, Xiangyuan Zhu, Zehui Xiao, Kehua Guo
publish: IEEE Transactions on Multimedia
abstract: Blind image super-resolution (BSR) aims to reconstruct high-resolution (HR) images from low-resolution (LR) images with unknown degradations. Existing BSR methods mainly follow two pipelines: LR prior-based reconstruction and SR posterior-based reconstruction. However, LR prior-based methods are sensitive to degradation and lack correction, and SR posterior-based approaches face limitations in generalization. From the perspective of aggregating both LR priors and SR posteriors, we propose a multi-branch network, Dual Representation Aggregation Network (DRANet) that constructs degradation aware branch and deviation correction branch hierarchically. Moreover, the end-to-end case reveals that the interaction between two branches poses a significant challenge, leading to model collapse and poor performance. To address this issue, we propose Iterative Bi-level Optimization (IBO), which decouples the DRANet learning problem into three sub-problems for staged training. We first optimize the parameters of two branches separately. Once each branch converges to a satisfactory performance, we conduct joint training through an iterative approach. Experiments on several blind degradation settings show that our DRANet produces promising results compared to state-of-the-art BSR methods on various benchmarks. Ablation studies indicate the effectiveness of dual representation aggregation and the stability of IBO, compared to both single and end-to-end strategies. The code is available at: <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/para999/IBO</uri>.

title: Practical and Robust Privacy-Preserving K-Means Clustering Protocols With GPU Acceleration
authors: Tao Bai, Yuanqing Feng, Zhi Lu, Xueming Tang, Songfeng Lu, Yongquan Cui
publish: IEEE Transactions on Emerging Topics in Computing
abstract: Privacy-preserving machine learning allows for the protection of the original data's privacy while training models. In recent years, researchers have implemented machine learning algorithms using secure multi-party computation (MPC) techniques, with clustering being one of the key applications. However, current privacy-preserving clustering algorithms still face challenges regarding execution speed and communication overhead. In this paper, we propose a new three-party GPU-accelerated privacy-preserving K-means clustering algorithm aimed at improving computational efficiency in distributed settings. We design a three-party K-means algorithm and optimize subprotocols such as the truncation protocol and the constant-round comparison protocol to reduce communication latency. Additionally, we leverage the powerful capabilities of Graphics Processing Units (GPUs) to enhance the execution efficiency of the algorithm, particularly for intensive distance computation and centroid updates. Finally, we propose a privacy-preserving clustering quality evaluation protocol to address the parameter selection issue (determining the optimal number of clusters $K$) in privacy-preserving K-means. Experimental results on the synthetic datasets demonstrate that our method achieves a $137\times$–$221\times$ improvement in efficiency compared to the CPU-based protocol of Mohassel et al., and a $5.7\times$–$11.2\times$ speedup over the CPU-based state-of-the-art implementation by Wei et al..

title: An Enhanced Elliptically-Deformed Folded Waveguide for E-Band Traveling Wave Tubes with Hot-Test Validation
authors: Han Lai, Fujun Xiao, Zhihao Xing, Mengjie Yuan, Zhaoyun Duan, Yubin Gong, Dun Lu, Huarong Gong
publish: IEEE Electron Device Letters
abstract: This letter presents a novel elliptically-deformed folded waveguide (ED-FWG) with enhanced electromagnetic characteristics, holding promise for high-performance traveling wave tubes (TWTs) in future broadband wireless communication and radar systems. Compared to the conventional structure, the proposed ED-FWG exhibits improved interaction impedance and higher circuit gain per unit length, thereby facilitating device miniaturization. Meanwhile, the ED-FWG exhibits both flat dispersion and high reflectivity, contributing to a broader bandwidth. It provides greater design flexibility and involves no additional fabrication complexity. An E-band prototype based on this structure was fabricated and tested. The device achieves a saturated output power exceeding 130 W and a peak power of 170 W in the 71-76 GHz band. The gain exceeds 52.5 dB, reaching a maximum of 56.2 dB. These results validate the ED-FWG as an effective solution for enhancing TWTs. Its design principles could be applied to other higher-frequency bands, especially those that demand high power, high gain, and compact power sources.

title: A Communication Failure and Cyber-Attack Resilient Wide-Area Out-of-Step Prediction Scheme Using Dynamic State Estimation
authors: Amir Dadashi, Shahin Riahinia, Amir Ameli, Hassan Naser, Mohsen Ghafouri
publish: IEEE Transactions on Power Systems
abstract: Out-of-step (OOS) prediction is essential for reducing mechanical and electrical stress on power system components. A reliable strategy involves using wide-area measurement systems (WAMS) to monitor the dynamic behavior of voltage angle differences across transmission lines and generator rotor angles. However, the limited availability of phasor measurement units (PMUs) presents a challenge, as voltage angle measurements are not available at all buses. Moreover, rotor angles cannot be directly measured and must be estimated. This paper proposes an OOS prediction scheme based on dynamic state estimation (DSE), which concurrently estimates both algebraic and dynamic states. By calculating the slip frequency and the acceleration of voltage angle differences, the electrical center's location is predicted by tracking its trajectory on the slip-acceleration plane. A similar approach is used to predict generator OOS based on the estimated rotor angles. Since the scheme depends on WAMS data, it is susceptible to cyber-threats such as false data injection attacks (FDIAs), denial-of-service attacks (DoSAs), and communication failures. To improve resilience, the proposed method integrates a penalized weighted least squares (PWLS) approach into an extended Kalman filter (EKF) for detecting and mitigating attacks and handling data loss. The scheme is validated on the IEEE 39-bus test system under various network topologies and attack scenarios.

title: Subarray-based Phase-less Angular Parameter Estimation for an Incoherently Distributed Source
authors: Zhe Li, Mingkang Hou, Ziyu Guo, Bo Hu
publish: IEEE Wireless Communications Letters
abstract: This letter proposes to leverage the subarray-based antenna array structure to estimate the angular parameters of an incoherently distributed source. It only needs to observe the received power and does not need the phase of the subarrays, where the number of antenna elements is set to increase by one across adjacent subarrays. An approximate expression for the second-order difference of the received power is derived first, assuming that the source has a symmetric distribution. Based on that, the nominal angle-of-arrival can be estimated without knowing the distribution. If the distribution is given, the angular spread can be estimated directly. Compared with the methods developed on fully digital arrays, the proposed method has much lower computational complexity. Meanwhile, the simulation results show that it can achieve higher angular spread estimation accuracy.

title: Preface to the Special Issue
authors: Danial Faghihi, Prashant K. Jha, Serge Prudhomme
publish: Computing in Science & Engineering
abstract: This special issue is meant to celebrate the lifetime achievements of J. Tinsley Oden (1936– 2023) in the course of his career. He is recognized by many as a pioneer in the field of computational mechanics, a term he coined himself early on, and as the founder of the Oden Institute for Computational Engineering and Sciences at The University of Texas at Austin.

title: SwarmRouter: Obstacle-Avoiding Routing for Droplet Swarms on Active-Matrix Digital Microfluidic Biochips
authors: Ziyang Chen, Zhiwen Yu, Haoyang Xu, Bin Guo, Chunyu Chang, Sizhao Li, Tsung-Yi Ho, Hanbin Ma, Xing Huang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computer-Aided Design of Integrated Circuits and Systems
abstract: Digital microfluidic biochips (DMFBs), renowned for their programmable and digital manipulations of droplets, have been extensively applied in the automation of bioassays. However, constrained by factors such as electrode size, quantity, and addressing methods, conventional DMFBs face challenges in deploying large-scale biochemical applications. In contrast, the next-generation active-matrix digital microfluidic biochips (AM-DMFs), leveraging their powerful capabilities in cross-addressing and parallel droplet manipulations, have emerged as a key alternative platform for high-throughput bioassays. Nevertheless, the execution of large-scale bioassays on the AM-DMF platforms poses significant challenges for droplet routing. For example, in single-cell proteomics assays, to facilitate high-throughput processing of numerous samples, hundreds and thousands of split droplets need to be transported in parallel to designated areas while avoiding on-chip obstacles. This gives rise to the large-scale obstacle-avoiding routing problem, which existing approaches prove inadequate to effectively address. In this paper, we propose the first obstacle-avoiding routing method called SwarmRouter for large-scale droplet swarms, which enables efficient path planning for droplets while ensuring conflict-free and deadlock-free operations. The proposed method includes the following key techniques: 1) A geometric-temporal coupled preprocessing strategy integrating droplet spacing optimization and priority scheduling for conflict and deadlock elimination, 2) a path preselection strategy for both path length reduction and conflict avoidance, and 3) a timing-driven parallel routing method with dynamic checkpoints for waiting time minimization. Experimental results on an ACXEL AM16K biochip system demonstrate that SwarmRouter successfully realizes conflict-free path planning for all given droplets, while achieving significant reductions of 25.13% in total path length, 19.6% in maximum arrival time, and an improvement of 12.63% in average droplet velocity compared to a baseline method.

title: Co-Divide: Multi-Granularity Consistency Learning for Remote Sensing Image Retrieval under Severe Noisy Labels
authors: Yeyun Wu, Xiaolin Gao, Junsheng Wang, Tiantian Gong
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Cross-domain content-based remote sensing image retrieval (CBRSIR) under noisy environments represents a promising yet challenging research direction, focusing on knowledge transfer from noise-corrupted source domain to unlabeled target domain. However, existing domain adaptation methods often overlook the characteristic of remote sensing images (RSIs) exhibiting large intra-class variance and small inter-class variance, failing to meet the requirements adequately. To address this issue, we propose a novel framework called CDMCL (co-divide and multi-granularity consistency learning). First, we introduce a dual-model collaborative divide (co-divide) and correction (co-correction) strategy, where two heterogeneous Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM) interactively separate clean samples and rectify noisy labels via neighbor consistency. Second, we innovatively employ a graph neural network (GNN) module to extract domain-invariant features. Finally, we design a strongly augmented and weakly augmented consistency learning strategy in the target domain, combining maximum mean discrepancy (MMD) and prototype constraints to achieve multi-granularity feature alignment. Additionally, a contrastive learning mechanism is introduced to further enhance the discriminative capability of target domain features. Extensive experiments demonstrate that CDMCL significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods on multiple cross-domain remote sensing retrieval benchmarks. In particular, CDMCL achieves a 19.98% performance improvement on the DB2 dataset under a severe noisy scenario with 60% noisy labels.

title: Cover Page
authors: N/A
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer, Data Sciences and Applications (ACDSA)

title: Analysis of Channel Congestion for Vehicular Network in High Vehicle Density Environment
authors: Bhupendra Dhakad, Sadhana Mishra, Shailendra Singh Ojha, Laxmi Shrivastava
publish: 2025 International Conference on Electrical, Communication, and Computing Technologies (iCONECCT)
abstract: Vehicular Ad-Hoc Network (VANET) is a collection of lots of mobile vehicles. These mobile vehicles are communicating with one another to perform diverse functions to avoid accidents, reduced carbon emissions, and gain information about the surrounding environment. VANET could also notify bicyclist in the street and a pedestrian in the crosswalk and reduces pedestrian-related accidents. Paper constrains the performance parameter metrics and calculates the data congestion level on channel by considering the message transmission rate. The road map is designed with the help of SUMO NETEDIT 1.12.0 software and the performance parameter matrices are calculated by NS2 by considering the IEEE802.11p standard. The performance parameter matrices include throughput, end-to-end delay, count of drop packets, and count of received packets.

title: Safety Risk Analysis and Control of Unattended Environmental Screening Test System
authors: Huajun Wang, Yu Yi, Di Wang, Yi Jian, Haisha Qu, Tao Zhang
publish: 2025 International Conference on Mechatronics, Automation and Intelligent Control (MAIC)
abstract: The unattended environmental screening test system, characterized by its labor-saving, efficiency-improving, and cost-reducing features, has been applied to the development and production of aerospace assemblies and board-level products. This paper analyzes four types of factors affecting system stability during operation and proposes safety risk control methods from four aspects to enhance the safety and stability of the unattended environmental screening test system, ensuring the security of equipment and products.

title: Remote-Controlled Elevator Car Real-Time Monitoring and Control System: Integrating Detection, Regulation, and Response
authors: Lu Zhou, Jia-Wei Li, Zi-Hui Yang, RongFeng Xie, Shu-Tong Chen, Shengfeng Lin
publish: 2025 International Conference on Mechatronics, Automation and Intelligent Control (MAIC)
abstract: Addressing the microbial contamination issues in elevator cars as high-frequency contact enclosed spaces, along with the inefficiencies, chemical residues, and lack of intelligent control in traditional manual disinfection methods, this study designed and implemented an intelligent disinfection system for elevator cabins that integrates multimodal sensing, adaptive decision-making, and precise execution. Four core sensors are employed for multi-dimensional environmental perception, and the Kalman filter algorithm is used for data fusion, keeping sensor data errors below 5%. In terms of disinfection strategy, the classic Chick-Watson microbial inactivation kinetics model is combined with real-time environmental parameter feedback to construct an adaptive disinfection decision engine. For execution control, UVC-LED light sources and a PID feedback control algorithm are utilized for precise UV intensity control, with a closed-loop feedback mechanism established through real-time monitoring by UV radiation intensity sensors. A multi-layer safety interlock mechanism is implemented, with personnel detection response delays <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$<100 \text{ms}$</tex> and ozone concentration controlled <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\leqslant 0.05 \text{ppm}$</tex>, meeting the DB4403/T 412-2024 standard requirements. A cloud platform based on the B/S architecture is developed, using the MQTT protocol for secure bidirectional communication, supporting remote monitoring and control. This system provides an efficient and safe disinfection solution for high-frequency elevator use, helping to enhance public health safety standards in cities.

title: A Multi-Feature Analysis Model For Detecting Programmer Fatigue Through Cognitive And Behavioural Activities
authors: D. Thamizhisai, S. Reshma, R. Sangeetha, J. Sruthi Lumina, K. Satheesh Kumar
publish: 2025 International Conference on Electrical, Communication, and Computing Technologies (iCONECCT)
abstract: In recent years, burnout and fatigue have appeared as severe psychosomatic conditions among programmers or software developers. The main causes for the burnout of programmers were continuous workload, protracted screen exposure, and challenging project phases affecting the programmer's performance. They propose a fatigue detection system that analyzes eye movements, facial micro expressions, keystroke dynamics, posture, and coding behavior analysis to evaluate the fatigue level of the programmer. The stress-based dataset, which is pretrained, serves as the initial model, thus further updated by the calibrate library of python to adapt to individual programmer's behavior and reduce false predictions. The evaluation was performed by the machine learning and deep learning models including logistic regression, XGboost, random forest, Convolutional Neural Network, Recurrent Neural Network, Gaussian Naïve Bayes and Convolutional Neural Network - Long Short-Term Memory. The WESAD stress dataset was used for the benchmark comparison to authenticate the robustness. The highest accuracy is achieved by the CNN-LSTM model of 97.6% and provides real-time suggestion to improve the programmer's working conditions and to develop a healthy environment.

title: Blueseg: A Memory Light Lane Detection Network Using Blueprint Separable Convolution
authors: Madiha Shabir Shaikh, Sadia Muniza Faraz
publish: 2026 1st International Conference on Innovations in Information and Communication Technologies (IICT)
abstract: The steady increase in both population and vehicular density worldwide requires the safeguarding of drivers from road accidents. For addressing this challenge, we propose a computationally efficient lane boundary estimation architecture built upon semantic segmentation. For this study, two networks, BlueSeg-L and BlueSeg-S, are designed, employing convolutional as well as blueprint separable convolutional layers for feature extraction. The proposed networks have been compared with benchmark networks. BlueSeg-S outperformed the benchmark SegNet with improvements of 6.85% in Intersection Over Union (IoU) and 3.82% in Dice Score, while BlueSeg-L and BlueSeg-S achieved reductions of 41% and 85% in computational parameters, respectively. Owing to high segmentation accuracy and low computational parameters, our networks can be deployed in designing systems which demand reduced computations.

title: Comparative Analysis of XGBoost and LightGBM Models for Glucose Metabolism Disorder Prediction Using IQR-Based Data Preprocessing
authors: Deepak Singla, Reena Mehla, Ekta Dureja, Shipra, Shiraz Khurana
publish: 2026 2nd International Conference on Cognitive Computing in Engineering, Communications, Sciences and Biomedical Health Informatics (IC3ECSBHI)
abstract: The worldwide prevalence of Glucose Metabolism Disorder (GMD) continues to rise as a major health problem which affects developing countries at an accelerated rate. The solution to this problem involves using advanced machine learning techniques which help doctors detect GMD earlier and improve their predictive accuracy. The implementation of these models faces challenges because researchers lack effective methods to choose suitable algorithms and maintain consistent prediction accuracy. The dataset preprocessing process stands as a fundamental requirement for this work. The data cleaning process follows scaling procedures and IQR methods for outlier treatment. The performance metrics of XGBoost and LightGBM algorithms show significant differences when evaluating their accuracy and precision and recall and F1 score and AUC score results. The combination of XGBoost with IQR preprocessing produces excellent results for accuracy and precision and recall measurements. The tool provides valuable capabilities for GMD prediction through its accurate results. The performance of LightGBM depends on the chosen indicator which demonstrates the need for detailed evaluation of application requirements when selecting a model. The development of dependable GMD prediction models requires complete data preprocessing and multiple validation steps to ensure their effectiveness in actual deployment.

title: Dynamic Performance Comparison of PI and Predictive Controllers for Bidirectional DC-DC Converters in Hybrid Energy Storage Systems
authors: Srinivas Punna, Deepak Bondaday, Prasanta Behera, Mohit Saxena, Anoop Lamba
publish: 2026 4th Odisha International Conference on Electrical Power Engineering, Communication and Computing Technology (ODICON)
abstract: Modern electric grid systems may efficiently include energy storage systems (ESSs), electric loads, and renewable energy sources (RES). As a result, DC microgrid technology has gained popularity. In this study, proportional integral (PI) and model predictive control (MPC) are used to assess multiple input bidirectional DC-DC converters (MIPC) for hybrid energy storage systems (HESS) in DC microgrid applications. Due to their opposing properties, the battery and supercapacitor (SC) make the perfect HESS combination. This MIPC encourages energy interchange between storage devices and decouples battery and SC power regulation. A controller intended for DC microgrid applications was altered to create a single controller that could perform both HESS charging and discharging functions. Batteries have an extremely sluggish rate of charge and discharge when employed as energy storage. Because SC has a high-power density and can respond quickly to abrupt changes in load, the battery is under current stress and has a shorter lifespan. Because SC cannot provide power to the load for an extended period of time, it cannot be employed alone as an energy storage device. The modelling results show that MPC control systems perform better than PI control systems. The suggested MPC system outperformed the conventional PI approach in terms of restoring DC grid voltage to step changes in PV power and load demand.

title: Power Splitting Technique for PV Integrated Grid Connected Hybrid Storage System
authors: Shyama Sundar Padhi, Pravat Kumar Ray, Sanjeeb Mohanty, Prangya Parimita Pradhan
publish: 2026 4th Odisha International Conference on Electrical Power Engineering, Communication and Computing Technology (ODICON)
abstract: This paper proposes a smart grid-interactive hybrid energy management system (SGIHM) integrating photo-voltaic (PV) generation with a battery-supercapacitor hybrid storage system (BSHSS) for coordinated active power control.
