title: Simplified Dynamic Thermal Model of Contactless Power Transfer for Electric Buses
authors: Luigi Pio Di Noia, Renato Rizzo
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Intelligent Driving and Smart Transportation (IDST)
abstract: The recharge of electric vehicle using Inductive power transfer (IPT) could plays an important role for the correct energy management strategy of fleet of electric buses. Therefore, the software and the tools necessary for the energy management strategy require magnetic and thermal model of all the component adopted, starting from the charging system until to the on-board powertrain. The paper analyzes the use of a simplified dynamic model able to obtain a fast and accurate thermal behavior of the pads adopted in IPT system. Some numerical simulations are carried out with finite element analysis and Matlab to verify the goodness of the proposed approach.

title: Half Title Page
authors: N/A
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Intelligent Driving and Smart Transportation (IDST)

title: Investigative Evaluation of the Multi-Carrier Pulse Width Modulation Techniques for Single-Phase 15-Level MLI Topology
authors: P. Anjaneya Vara Prasad, C Dhanamjayulu
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Smart Power, Energy, Renewables, and Transportation (SPERT)
abstract: Multilevel Inverter (MLI) topologies are the most commonly utilized converter topology in industries for medium voltage applications at present. These MLI topologies play a crucial role in the power management of the industries by being the position of main integrating aspect of the stored backup energy. The importance of the MLI in the industries is provides more challenging issues like making controlled strategic techniques, providing the smooth operation, and reducing harmonics in the system. The best possible establishment of a solution to these issues is to provide an appropriate Pulse Width Modulation (PWM) technique for that MLI topology. The different multicarrier PWM techniques are considered for evaluating the performance of the MLI topology. And these methods are analyzed and compared with different aspects, such as voltage and its Total Harmonic Distortion (THD), with different modulation index values. The simulation outcomes of the MLI topology with different PWM techniques are obtained by using MATLAB/Simulink software.

title: Solar Powered Wireless Charging System for Electric Vehicles
authors: Chintan R. Mehta, Vivek J. Patal, Amar Kumar Barik
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Smart Power, Energy, Renewables, and Transportation (SPERT)
abstract: This work proposes a static wireless charging system using resonant inductive coupling for electric vehicle (EV) powered by solar energy, in order to achieve the global sustainable goal with a carbon neutral process. It also overcomes the compatibility issues of the charging guns in different EVs with the ports of wired charging stations. As solar power generation is intermittent in nature, the proposed system is simulated in MATLAB for four different climatic scenarios and the performances were analyzed to confirm the charging requirement of EV. Artificial hummingbird algorithm is employed with incremental conductance based maximum power point tracking to extract optimum solar energy from photovoltaic arrays. The available solar power is used to energize the primary coil of charging station through a high frequency DC to AC converter. The receiving coil on EV induce the voltage wirelessly and further converted to DC for fueling EV battery. An auxiliary battery storage unit is included in primary side with solar arrays, to support the charging process uninterruptedly. The performances were compared and reported in this work for four different cases based on availability of solar generations to imitate all time EV charging support.

title: Reducing Energy Consumption in LoRa Networks for Smart Agriculture
authors: Gaurav Jangir, Neeraj Kumar Gupta, Tanishk Patodi, Ghanapriya Singh
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Smart Power, Energy, Renewables, and Transportation (SPERT)
abstract: For modern agriculture, it is critical to monitor environmental conditions efficiently since it requires major manpower. This work presents a smart agriculture system that monitors temperature, soil moisture, and humidity in real-time to support farming, resource and power optimization. For minimizing energy consumption, there has been employment of a hierarchical network architecture with a three-tier layout for large farmlands and a two-layer layout for smaller farmlands. Long Range (LoRa) technology is used to ensure long-range, lowpower communication. The results of the Simulation demonstrate that the system provides high scalability and energy efficiency. The short-range configuration covers of 97.9 meters with 0.517 mJ per transmission, but in case of long-range, it covers 12.3 km with 5.16 J per transmission. The data compression reduces energy usage by 26.98 %, and the layered network design additionally reduces 86.57 % When combined together, these achieve up to 97.79 % energy savings for 5000 acres, stabilizing around <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{9 5. 5 2 \%}$</tex> for larger areas. The architecture offers a low-power solution that is suitable for both small and large-scale smart farming applications.

title: Scientific Foundations of an Intelligent Method for Quality Assessment and Monitoring of Information Content and Digital Security of Public Resources of Educational Organizations
authors: Maxim S. Kravchenko, Andrey V. Averchenkov, Sergey A. Sheptunov, Dmitriy A. Averchenkov
publish: 2025 International Conference on Quality Management, Transport and Information Security, Information Technologies (QM&amp;TIS&amp;IT)
abstract: The article considers the procedure of analysis of public resources of educational organizations for compliance with the established requirements of the state and actual needs of users. The existing methods are critically analyzed and their key advantages and disadvantages are identified. A scientific justification for the creation of a new intellectual method of quality assessment and monitoring and an algorithm of work for the creation of a new tool are proposed. The implementation of the method will allow educational organizations to minimize the risk of information distortion or loss and reduce the risk of cyberattacks, ensuring the reliability of digital resources for users.

title: Development of Intelligent Information Systems to Ensure the Safety of the Transport Infrastructure of a Metropolis
authors: Elena Yu. Narusova, Vladimir G. Struchalin, Ekaterina L. Koltsova, Pavel S. Polyansky, Mikhail R. Pleshakov, Gleb E. Druzhinin
publish: 2025 International Conference on Quality Management, Transport and Information Security, Information Technologies (QM&amp;TIS&amp;IT)
abstract: The article is devoted to the problem of ensuring technical safety of complex urban transport systems with the help of automated systems for monitoring and equipment control. An example of ensuring the safety of continuous and vertical transport equipment for transport hubs of a metropolis is considered. Using statistical analysis tools and reliability theory, an analysis of the main causes of safety violations in the use of equipment at metro transfer stations is carried out. Recommendations are given for the development of intelligent information systems for dispatching and ensuring safety at transport infrastructure facilities.

title: Radiation-Hardened, Low-Power 11T Sram Cell with Single Bitline Configuration
authors: Shahneela Jamal Kidwai, Naushad Alam
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Multimedia, Signal Processing and Communication Technologies (IMPACT)
abstract: This work presents a single bit-line 11 transistor SRAM cell (SB11TSC) topology that is resistant to radiation. The cell uses stacked transistors in both the pull-up and pulldown branches to stop charge collection and improve immunity to single-event upsets (SEUs). The single bit-line configuration also cuts down on switching activity and power loss. The simulation shows that the proposed design has a read access time of 8.7 ps and a write delay of 316 ps. This implies that it has a very competitive read speed but has slower write speed due to single bit-line. The read and write power drops to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$276 \mu \mathrm{W}$</tex> and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$287 \mu \mathrm{W}$</tex> respectively. Leakage power is only <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.115 \mu \mathrm{W}$</tex> which shows its potential for low-power applications. The proposed cell has a critical charge (Qc) of 23.957 fC and can withstand a peak transient current of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 4 7} \boldsymbol{\mu} \mathbf{A}$</tex> during particles strike at the sensitive node. This demonstrates that it can handle SEUs better than the AS8T and AS10T designs considered for comparison. These results show that the suggested 11T single bit-line SRAM strikes a good balance between power, performance and radiation tolerance making it a good choice for memory applications in space and defense.

title: Reliability-Constrained Optimal Placement of Reclosers and Distributed Generators in Distribution Networks Using the Pelican Optimization Algorithm
authors: Sanjoy Kumar Saha, Swati Mukherjee, Disha Dey
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Multimedia, Signal Processing and Communication Technologies (IMPACT)
abstract: To make sure that the power system is reliable, it is vital to put protective devices and distributed generators (DGs) at the right places in radial feeders. One of the goals of using distributed generation in distribution networks is to make the system more reliable. This research employs a pelican optimization technique to determine the ideal placement plan for reclosers and distributed generators in radial distribution networks. The optimization process uses a composite reliability index as the goal. Simulations are conducted using two realistic distribution systems to verify the efficacy of the suggested strategy. Additionally, comparative research concerning genetic algorithms is also undertaken.

title: Copyright Page
authors: N/A
publish: 2025 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Embedded Systems, and Renewable Energy (AIESRE)

title: From Stealth to Signals: Practical Cyber-Attack Emulation and Lightweight Detection for Smart Grids
authors: Indu Sekhar Samanta, Pravat Kumar Rout, Sarthak Mohanty, Konneh Boakai Zinnah
publish: 2026 4th Odisha International Conference on Electrical Power Engineering, Communication and Computing Technology (ODICON)
abstract: This paper presents an educational yet practically grounded mini-lab for smart-grid cybersecurity built entirely in vanilla MATLAB, requiring no external toolboxes. We cover four current threat surfaces and corresponding lightweight defenses: stealth false-data injection attacks (FDIA) on DC/AC state estimation; denial-of-service (DoS) / packet loss on telemetered measurements; load-altering attacks on automatic generation control (AGC) via electricvehicle (EV) charging demand; and timesynchronization/timestamp manipulation of IEC-61850 GOOSE messages. We implement weighted least-squares (WLS) bad-data detection (BDD), hardening via a single trusted PMU channel with a CUSUM residual monitor, Gauss-Newton AC state estimation with linearized stealth FDIA, a single-area AGC model under EV load perturbations, a timing-skew and inter-arrival CUSUM detector for GOOSE, and a from-scratch Extreme Learning Machine (ELM) intrusion detector from residual/arrival statistics. On the AC estimator, the baseline <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\chi^{2}$</tex> residual energy is 1.3100, while a crafted stealth FDIA yields 1.3077. In the AGC case, an EV load-altering attack produces a frequency nadir of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{- 0. 5 0 0 H z}$</tex>. For GOOSE timing manipulation, the inter-arrival CUSUM alarms at <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{t} \approx \mathbf{3 0. 0 0 s}$</tex>.

title: Comprehensive Analysis of Design Parameters, Configurations, and Energy Management in Autonomous Unified Distributed Generation Systems
authors: Sachin Chaturvedi, Omveer Singh, Pawan Kumar Kushwaha
publish: 2026 2nd International Conference on Cognitive Computing in Engineering, Communications, Sciences and Biomedical Health Informatics (IC3ECSBHI)
abstract: Due to a scarce power supply, current existing energy systems (ES) are unable to fully meet the exponentially increasing energy demand brought on by industrialization and population increase. Additionally, ES has an important impact on the natural environment. Autonomous unified distributed generation (AUDG) is a good blend of non-renewable and renewable energy sources that take into account the advantages of the system, such as enhanced overall system reliability, reduced emissions, and lower implementation and maintenance costs, as compared to ES. Therefore, this article offers a review of an AUDG's design parameters, configurations, sizing techniques, energy management, storage technologies, and sensitivity analysis aspects that need to be taken into account when creating and executing an AUDG. The designer can choose the optimal AUDG configuration, suitable energy management, and AUDG mode of operation with the help of this research. The choice of several design characteristics and sensitivity parameters for AUDG performance evaluation is also included.

title: Accelerating Dqn Convergence With Behavioral Cloning: a Performance Analysis
authors: Kedar Maheshwari, Samarth Bhavsar, Rebakah Geddam, Hemant Ghayvat
publish: 2025 International Conference on Electrical, Communication, and Computing Technologies (iCONECCT)
abstract: Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) agents are often plagued with significant sample inefficiencies, needing millions of interactions before meaningful policy learning takes place. Imitation Learning (IL) from human demonstrations is a potential means of ameliorating these sample inefficiencies by providing the learning agent with a strong initial policy. This paper presents a complete experimental framework for analysing this hybrid agent type in a custom-built grid-world game environment. We detail the environment dynamics, implement a data logging pipeline, and present a formal 50 -feature state space representation. We analyse the 21720 sample human dataset to train a Behavioural Cloning (BC) model, achieving a validation accuracy of 82.35%. We initialise a Deep Q-network (DQN) policy with the pre-trained BC model. We then analyse the hybrid agent compared to a randomly initialised baseline agent. Results show that while the hybrid agent demonstrates an advantage early on, all agents converge to a similar policy with high-variance outcomes after approximately 2000 episodes, suggesting the human policy advantage was temporal under these training conditions.

title: Design, Analysis and Control of a High-Efficiency Photovoltaic System Using a Three-Phase Interleaved Boost Converter with Composite Energy Storage Devices for Electric Vehicle Charging
authors: Konneh Boakai Zinnah, Sarthak Mohanty, Shubhranshu Mohan Parida, Buddhadeva Sahoo, Pravat Kumar Rout, Indu Sekhar Samanta
publish: 2026 4th Odisha International Conference on Electrical Power Engineering, Communication and Computing Technology (ODICON)
abstract: This paper presents the design and control of a high-efficiency three-phase interleaved DC-DC boost converter (3P-IBC) integrated with a composite energy storage device (CESD) for photovoltaic (PV) applications. The proposed converter employs 120° phase-shifted interleaving to achieve ripple cancellation, uniform current distribution, and reduced electromagnetic interference (EMI), ensuring continuous conduction mode (CCM) operation. A cascaded dual-loop proportional-integral (PI) control strategy regulates inductor current and stabilizes the DC-link voltage under dynamic irradiance and load variations. The coordinated operation of the battery-supercapacitor CESD enhances transient performance and voltage stability during power fluctuations. Simulation results demonstrate a DC-bus voltage regulation at 100 V with an efficiency of approximately 99.3 %, significantly outperforming conventional, quadratic, and two-phase interleaved converters. The proposed 3P-IBC topology thus offers an effective, compact, and reliable power-processing solution for medium-to high-power PV-based DC microgrids and electric vehicle charging applications.

title: Transformer-IoTNet: A Deep Learning Framework for Anomaly and Threat Detection in High-Density IoT Networks
authors: M. Ravinder, Sudha Rani V
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Emerging Trends in Engineering and Medical Sciences (ICETEMS)
abstract: In this research work, a hybrid deep learning model is proposed for anomaly and threat detection in the Internet of Things (IoT) network. The IoT ecosystem features leading technology, as well as increased access to applications; however, due to the heterogeneity of the network, it can be vulnerable to anomalies and sophisticated cyberattacks. Traditional Intrusion Detection (TIDS) models are unable to detect emerging threats on high volumes of streaming data. One more drawback of existing models is their limited capability for feature representation and their failure to detect emerging threats. These challenges have been addressed by the proposed Transformer IoTNet (TIoTNet) deep learning model. The proposed TIoTNet model can detect effective anomalies and threats in dense IoT environments. The proposed model leverages the self-attention mechanism of transformer architectures to identify high dependencies, temporal correlations, and complex anomaly patterns in the IoT network. The false alarms have decreased due to a multi-stage feature extraction pipeline and contextual learning. The Transformer-IoTNet model has been evaluated on benchmark IoT-enabled datasets, where it validates high performance compared to conventional machine learning and deep learning TIDS models. The experiment was performed on the DriveHQ cloud, and the results show significant improvements in detection accuracy (98.87%), precision (98.29%), recall (96.93%), and F1 score (97.48%). The Proposed Transformer IoTNet framework also exhibits robustness in all diverse network operating conditions. Finally, the TIOTNet model has the potential to detect anomalies with transformer architectures for next-generation IoT networks.

title: Research on Autonomous Driving Route Planning Method Based on Deep Reinforcement Learning
authors: Yichen Jin, Wudeng Wang
publish: 2025 International Conference on Mechatronics, Automation and Intelligent Control (MAIC)
abstract: This paper introduces a hybrid deep reinforcement learning path planning framework (DRL-PP) for autonomous vehicles. It is designed to be efficient, safe and verifiable in complex and dynamic environments. This framework is built on a hierarchical action representation: the policy network provides multi-step reference path points, and at the lower level, MPC/PID is used to ensure that things are physically possible. The reward function adopts a combinatorial design and incorporates distancebased cubic boosters to amplify the learning signal when approaching the target. The training strategy employs a dualnetwork setup, experience replay, and a cautious <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\varepsilon$</tex>-cooling plan to enhance convergence stability and sample utilization. During the operation process, it adds action shielding and constraint projection based on control barrier functions or quadratic programming for real-time safety repair. Compared with benchmark systems such as DQN and Acactor -Critic on the unified grid obstacle simulation platform, this method has obvious advantages in terms of success rate, collision rate, path smoothness and sample efficiency (example results: success rate 94.2 %, collision rate 3.1 %, and the number of exploration iterations required to achieve stable performance is approximately <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$3.2 \times 10^{4}$</tex>).

title: Precise Geomagnetic Localization for Vehicles Based on CA-HLSTM
authors: Hu Rui, Lu Yi, Yuan Hong, Wei Dongyan
publish: 2025 International Ubiquitous Positioning, Indoor Navigation and Location-Based Services Conference (UPINLBS)
abstract: Precise vehicle localization is critical across numerous industries. Despite significant advances in satellitebased techniques, GNSS signals remain vulnerable to interference in environments such as underground parking garages and urban canyons. By leveraging geomagnetic signals that are less susceptible to such interference, geomagnetic positioning can continuously provide accurate and stable localization in complex scenarios. Traditional approaches have been widely applied to vehicle localization, however, during driving, vehicle speed varies continuously, causing geomagnetic signals sampled at a fixed rate to become “shape-distorted” when compared with reference maps, thereby degrading sequence matchability and leading to positioning errors. To address the above issue, we propose the CA-HLSTM-based geomagnetic localization method. Our approach directly learns the relationship between geomagnetic sequences acquired at varying speeds and geographic locations using neural networks, while incorporating a multi-scale architecture and a Convolution - Attention module, which enables staged learning of local features and global trends, meanwhile enhances salient local segments to achieve more accurate sequence-to-position prediction. We build two real-world datasets covering diverse environments and driving speeds for experimentation. Experimental results show that CA-HLSTM achieves a mean positioning error of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1. 0 6 ~ m}$</tex> indoors and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1. 7 3 ~ m}$</tex> outdoors.

title: Design and Implementation of a Real-Time Digital Twin for Power Transformer Protection Systems
authors: P. Sivakumar, D. Lochana, V. Meenaroshini, M. Kaleeswari
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Multimedia, Signal Processing and Communication Technologies (IMPACT)
abstract: Protection of power transformers plays a vital role in the reliability of the grid, but conventional differential relays tend to take a pounding from current transformer (CT) saturation, noise, and incipient fault sensitivity. This article introduces a Real-Time Digital Twin (DT)-driven transformer protection system that combines physics-based modelling with an Unscented Kalman Filter (UKF) for precise state estimation and noise-resistant fault detection. The DT platform integrates differential current, negative sequence, and impedance trajectory analyses to find interturn faults early in real time, making conventional offline diagnostics an active protective measure. Simulation of a 100 MVA, 220/33 kV transformer model in MATLAB/Simulink proved 7-10x times sensitivity improvement in interturn faults (1-2% vs. 10-15%), 85% lower misoperations, and 18 dB signal-to-noise improvement via UKFbased reconstruction. The system had 180 us latency and 25 % CPU usage, validating real-time feasibility. This work lays a basis for self-aware, intelligent protection systems that learn dynamically based on transformer health, which will lead to next-generation smart grid protection architectures.

title: Diabetes Diagnosis, Risk Assessment and Prognostic Modelling through Machine
authors: Sarthak Pandey, Jaya Pandey, Sumita Mishra, Pallavi Asthana, Vivek Srivastava
publish: 2026 International Conference on Computing, Sciences and Communications (ICCSC)
abstract: Diabetes mellitus is often termed as ‘silent disease’ due to its insidious onset and slow-paced symptoms in the initial stages, often characterized by hyperglycemia, causing the persistent increased levels of blood sugar. This disease is not diagnosed easily in the initial stages, and high blood sugar levels cause the impairment of vital organs such as kidneys, nerves, and eyes. Many parameters, such as LDL cholesterol, Glycohemoglobin, Insulin levels, dietary fibre, total fat component, and indirect features like BMI (Body Mass Index), Body weight, waist circumference, sleep duration and exercising pattern are effective to predict the probability of occurrence of Diabetes mellitus. A dataset of over ten thousand was trained and tested on various machine Learning models such as PSVM, Decision Trees, Ensemble Trees, Naïve Bayes, and KNN for accuracy, exactness, and sensitivity using the Bayesian optimisation for hyperparameter tuning. Naïve Baye predicted the outcome with the highest accuracy of 91 %, whereas ensemble trees with 74 % sensitivity are effective for variable features. Thus, the results prove that diabetes mellitus can be predicted by regularly monitoring the direct and indirect features of a person.

title: Radiomics-Driven Classification of Lung Cancer Subtypes Using Ensemble Machine Learning
authors: Amay Annasamudram, Pranav Kulkarni
publish: 2025 International Conference on Computational Intelligence, Security, and Artificial Intelligence (IntelliSecAI)
abstract: Lung cancer is one of the most common types of cancer in the world, killing thousands each year. This study aims to develop a novel multiclass machine learning model to differentiate between the different subtypes of lung cancer (adenocarcinoma, large cell carcinoma, small cell carcinoma, and squamous cell carcinoma) based on radiomic features derived from CT scans using Gray Level Co-occurrence Matrices (GLCM). Unlike other conventional approaches, the proposed model integrates ExtraTrees, LightGBM, and XGBoost algorithms to address class imbalance and variability in texture representation across subtypes. The dataset consisted of over 196,000 lung CT scan images from 355 patients with different kinds of cancer. Basic statistical features were extracted from the images, including mean, standard deviation, minimum, maximum, and median intensity, all to capture the overall variation of the image. The resulting features were then used to train the ensemble model. The model achieved an accuracy of 92.33 %, demonstrating improved performance when compared to other individual and integrated models commonly used in the field. The AUC score, a metric used to evaluate the performance of a classification model, was high for each of the four lung cancer subtypes, with values of 1.00 for large cell carcinoma, 0.99 for small cell carcinoma and squamous cell carcinoma, and 0.98 for adenocarcinoma. The analysis of the features indicated a potential for texture-derived metrics to contribute as potential noninvasive biomarkers, prompting further research in future studies.

title: Harnessing Machine Learning and Deep Learning to Predict Cumulative Abnormal Returns from CEO Behavioral Profiles
authors: Said Benlakhdar, Mohammed Rziza, Rachid Oulad Haj Thami
publish: 2025 International Conference on Advanced Technologies and Interdisciplinary Innovation (ICAT2I)
abstract: Mergers and acquisitions (M&A) are central to corporate growth, yet post-acquisition performance frequently shows declining cumulative abnormal returns (CAR). This study examines how CEO behavioral traits, particularly hubris and learning ability, shape CAR outcomes across 7,786 international M&A transactions conducted between 2012 and 2022. We assess the extent to which successive deal experience, deal completion, and time between deals interact with CEO characteristics to influence acquisition performance. Using econometric analysis enriched with machine learning methods and deep learning techniques, we capture both linear and non-linear patterns linking CEO traits to post-acquisition value creation. Although CEO characteristics account for a modest share of CAR variance, they exert significant effects on deal timing, success likelihood, and value outcomes. Hubristic CEOs display more aggressive acquisition behavior, while learning-oriented CEOs show improved performance over successive deals. These findings advance the understanding of the behavioral drivers of M&A performance and highlight the value of integrating executive behavioral assessment with data-driven predictive tools in strategic decision making.

title: Combining I-BSIF Fingerprints and FlexenTech for Reversible Medical Image Watermarking
authors: Brahim Ferik, Lakhdar Laimeche, Abdallah Meraoumia, Abdelkader Laouid, Khaled Chait
publish: 2025 International Conference on Recent Advances in Mathematics and Informatics (ICRAMI)
abstract: The rise of digital medical imaging in telemedicine raises concerns about image authenticity and patient privacy. Traditional watermarking methods struggle to achieve robust security and reliable patient authentication simultaneously. This study presents a novel blind reversible watermarking framework that combines orientation-guided Improved Binary Statistical Image Features (I-BSIF) with FlexenTech encryption to improve medical image security. The method utilizes advanced fingerprint feature extraction tailored to local ridge orientations, resulting in compact biometric signatures. These features undergo lightweight FlexenTech encryption before being embedded into medical images through spread-spectrum modulation in mid-frequency subbands of the Lifting Wavelet Transform. Experimental results demonstrate that rotation-invariant feature extraction preserves more than 99% similarity after a 270° rotation, with an average Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio (PSNR) exceeding 51 dB and a Structural Similarity Index (SSIM) of nearly 0.999, confirming the method's effectiveness in maintaining patient authentication and image integrity.

title: Multiwall Carbon Nanotubes-Integrated Fiber Microsphere Probe Light Intensity Sensor with Harmonic Vernier Effect
authors: Ying Wan, Sheng Zhang, Chen Jiang, Jianxin Ren, Jingkun Xu, Jianxiang Wen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Reliable and highly sensitive fiber-based light intensity sensors are increasingly demanded for integrated and miniaturized optical sensing platforms. In this work, we demonstrate a highly sensitive all-fiber light intensity sensor based on a hollow microsphere Fabry–Pérot (FP) cavity probe coated with multiwalled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs). Light intensity detection is realized through the photothermal conversion of MWCNTs and is further enhanced by a harmonic Vernier mechanism. The harmonic order of the FP cavity can be flexibly controlled by adjusting the air cavity length and the diameter of the microsphere. Microsphere FP cavities operating at the traditional, first-order harmonics, and second-order harmonics Vernier effect are fabricated experimentally. Temperature response measurements reveal that sensitivity increases significantly with the harmonic order. For the same harmonic order, a larger envelope-free spectral range of the Vernier spectrum leads to higher temperature sensitivity. A maximum temperature sensitivity of 0.392 nm/°C is achieved for the probe operating at the second order harmonic. By further functionalizing the probe with MWCNTs to realize photodetection, a light intensity sensitivity as high as 2.5 nm/mW is achieved. These results demonstrate that the hollow microsphere structure provides an excellent platform for realizing high-order harmonic Vernier effects, while the MWCNTs coating enhances the light intensity response, enabling a highly sensitive and integrated all-fiber light intensity sensor.

title: GranSSG: Correlating Volumetric Granularities for 3D Semantic Scene Graph Prediction
authors: Kaixiang Huang, Qifeng Zhang, Jin Wang, Jingru Yang, Yang Zhou, Jiao Yi, Guodong Lu, Shengfeng He
publish: IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics
abstract: Predicting 3D Semantic Scene Graphs (3DSSG) is vital for understanding complex scenes by constructing structured representations. Current methods struggle with significant granularity discrepancies among instances, often relying on features at a single scale, which hampers their ability to perceive and interact with differently sized instances. To tackle this challenge, we introduce GranSSG, a novel approach that integrates volumetric granular awareness into 3DSSG prediction. Central to GranSSG is the Volumetric Pooling block, which aggregates features from multiple instance volumes, enhancing the representation of instance patterns across different granularities. Complementing this, the Granularity Transformer block dynamically directs attention to instance features across various network layers, ensuring precise perception of instances regardless of their granularity. Furthermore, the Cross-Granularity Correlation Transformer block mitigates performance degradation in instance pair relationship prediction by adaptively fusing hybrid features from different granularities, providing a comprehensive representation of instance pairs. Extensive evaluations on the challenging 3DSSG benchmark demonstrate that GranSSG significantly enhances prediction performance, setting a new state-of-the-art in 3DSSG prediction.

title: AdaptiController: VR-Enhanced Fine Motor Assistance Through Finger Pressure Modulation
authors: Hangyu Zhou, Haotian Mao, Zixuan Guo, Yushi Wei, Yan Zhang, Xubo Yang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics
abstract: This paper explores finger pressure as a continuous implicit input modality to enhance interaction precision in virtual reality (VR). While motion controllers are widely adopted, their limitations in delicate operations remain a critical challenge. We investigate whether finger pressure signals from conventional VR controllers could offer advantages over traditional kinematic metrics for precision interaction.Through empirical studies, we demonstrate a robust relationship between pressure dynamics and task precision requirements, leading to a lightweight sigmoid-based model that leverages detected pressure to infer desired control granularity. In a comparative evaluation of video-scrubbing tasks, our adaptive method outperforms static sensitivity baselines in both task performance and subjective preference, without elevating cognitive load. Further validation via a VR sketching application demonstrates that our technique maintains task performance while reducing mental demand compared to manual control. Our findings reveal the untapped potential of pressure-based input to bridge coarse and fine-grained VR interactions, offering a path toward more versatile and intuitive input systems.

title: Building Better Community One Gelato at a Time
authors: Jay Lofstead
publish: Computing in Science & Engineering
abstract: Research communities are often relatively small and somewhat insular. This is not to say that the people are unfriendly to outsiders or newcomers, but more a reflection that people like to spend some time with long-term friends they see only a few times a year. Finding a way to encourage new community members to interact with more senior, long-term members requires a catalyst. Using something to equalize status and encourage people to talk can break down barriers and enable easier connection building and ultimately growing an integrated community rather than one split across generational lines.

title: Structural Design and Performance Optimization of Flexible Connection Structure of On-Load Tap Changer under Transient Electro-Thermo-Mechanical Coupling
authors: Yicong Zhou, Jinyan Duan, Ke Wang, Guoxin Mao, Gang Li, Jinhua Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Power Delivery
abstract: On-load tap changers (OLTCs) rely on flexible connection structures (FCSs) to maintain conductive circuits between moveable contacts. Stress concentration represents a primary cause of FCS mechanical failure, yet its behavior under transient electro-thermo-mechanical coupling conditions remains insufficiently addressed. This study develops a shape optimization method based on parametric curves and adaptive optimization to mitigate stress concentration in FCSs. Transient coupling stress analyses are performed for both fixed and moveable FCS types, and geometry-specific parametric curves are constructed according to the identified stress concentration locations and their underlying causes. Adaptive parameter optimization yields significant stress reductions in both cases. The analysis is further extended to multiple consecutive switching cycles, providing a more realistic assessment of FCS stress evolution under actual OLTC service conditions.

title: SHIELD: Blockchain-Enabled Lightweight Authentication Framework for Secure Wearable Health Monitoring in IoMT Environments
authors: Chao Wang, Xingsi Xue, Jing Wang, Huamao Jiang
publish: IEEE Journal of Biomedical and Health Informatics
abstract: Consumer health devices generate massive volumes of sensitive medical data requiring secure authentication mechanisms that accommodate the resource constraints of wearable sensors and portable diagnostic equipment. Traditional centralized authentication approaches in Internet of Medical Things (IoMT) environments suffer from single points of failure, privacy vulnerabilities, and scalability limitations when managing diverse health monitoring devices. This paper presents secure healthcare IoMT enhanced lightweight device authentication (SHIELD), a blockchain-based lightweight authentication framework designed for resource-constrained consumer health devices. The framework leverages blockchain's immutable and decentralized properties, combined with efficient elliptic curve cryptography, to ensure secure storage and verification of device identities while providing mutual authentication between health devices and medical data servers. Security analysis demonstrates that SHIELD satisfies twelve critical security properties, including decentralization, resistance to password guessing and replay attacks, perfect forward secrecy, and session key security. Performance evaluation reveals that SHIELD achieves computational efficiency at 9.837 milliseconds authentication latency, representing 31% improvement over previous best-performing schemes. The framework requires only 1384 bits of communication overhead and maintains minimal average delay times suitable for real-time health monitoring applications. Blockchain implementation analysis confirms practical deployment feasibility with 0.0356 MGas operational costs per authentication session.

title: A Novel Prescribed-Time Control Approach Under Unknown Control Gain and Mismatched Disturbance
authors: Guangtai Tian, Mehdi Golestani, Bin Li, Yongduan Song, Guangren Duan
publish: IEEE Transactions on Cybernetics
abstract: In this article, a prescribed-time output feedback controller is proposed for a class of uncertain nonlinear systems with unknown control coefficients and mismatched nonvanishing disturbances. Both unknown control coefficients and mismatched disturbances are tricky to address by the existing prescribed-time output feedback control frameworks. Differently, a novel prescribed-time control criterion in conjunction with Nussbaum functions is proposed, and prescribed-time stability is achieved. Furthermore, design methods for a state observer and a prescribed-time output feedback controller are developed. With the proposed control design, both the system output and observer errors are rigorously proved to converge to zero within a prescribed time. Moreover, the unified prescribed performance (UPP) of the system output and the satisfaction of output constraints are simultaneously achieved. Numerical simulations and experiments are provided to illustrate the effectiveness of the proposed control design.

title: IEEE Systems, Man, and Cybernetics Society Information
authors: N/A
publish: IEEE Transactions on Systems, Man, and Cybernetics: Systems

title: M2Net: Multimodal Multitask Mutual Learning for Anti-VEGF Efficacy Prediction
authors: Yang Wen, Ying Zeng, Lei Bi, Xinyu Zhao, Wuzhen Shi, Huazhu Fu, Bin Sheng
publish: IEEE Transactions on Medical Imaging
abstract: Age-related macular degeneration with abnormal blood vessel growth (neovascular AMD) is the leading cause of vision loss in elderly populations. While anti-VEGF injections are the standard treatment, they present financial burdens for patients and vary in effectiveness. Predicting treatment efficacy is therefore crucial for patient care. Current prediction methods fail to fully integrate information from different imaging techniques, typically focusing on either forecasting vision improvements or generating post-treatment images—but not both simultaneously. This approach overlooks the important relationship between these tasks. We present M2Net, a novel joint generation and classification network based on Multimodal Multitask Mutual learning, to simultaneously predict changes in visual acuity and generate post-treatment retinal images. M2Net employs a dual-branch structure that processes both fundus photographs and Optical Coherence Tomography (OCT) scans to improve prediction accuracy. Our framework includes two key innovations: the Multimodal Collaborative Treatment Efficacy Prediction module, which interacts the features between the two modalities and provides initial visual acuity change classification to guide the generation of post-treatment images; and the Pre-Post Treatment Image Joint Analysis module, which identifies both common and changing features between pre-treatment and post-treatment images to enhance prediction accuracy. To validate our approach, we created the dataset (MMPD) containing paired multimodal retinal images with corresponding visual acuity measurements. Experiments on the dataset demonstrate that M2Net achieves superior performance compared to existing methods, with a classification accuracy of 96.03%, an SSIM of 0.6377 on the OCT modality, and an SSIM of 0.8347 on the fundus modality. Our code will be available at https://github.com/zengying123/M2Net.

title: Extrusion Process Anomaly Detection via Batchnorm-Based Weakly Supervised Video Anomaly Detection
authors: Sua Han, Chang Hoon Hwang, Hong-In Won, Jinseok Jang, JongWook Lee, Seung-Hyun Jeong
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer, Data Sciences and Applications (ACDSA)
abstract: This study presents a method for detecting anomalies in the extrusion process used to manufacture coronary catheter tubes by utilizing video data captured during production. The proposed method is based on weakly supervised learning and leverages the statistical characteristics of feature vectors output from batch normalization (BN) layers to identify process anomalies. In particular, the model is trained to maximize the Mahalanobis distance between normal and abnormal candidate groups selected in the BN layer, enabling effective discrimination between normal and abnormal patterns. To validate the effectiveness of the proposed approach, a five-class process dataset was constructed by varying extrusion speed and temperature conditions. Experiments conducted with different frame units demonstrated outstanding performance: using 64-frame segments of 224 × 224 resolution video, the method achieved an AUC of 0.9991 and an accuracy of 99.16%, confirming its excellent capability in anomaly detection.

title: A Machine Learning Based Model for Exhaustion Attacks in Wireless Sensor Networks
authors: Wejdan Alghamdi, Abdullah Alomari
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer, Data Sciences and Applications (ACDSA)
abstract: With the increasing sophistication and advancements of Wireless Sensor Networks (WSNs), the risk of cyber threats and security breaches has grown significantly. Energy Exhaustion attacks are a type of cyber attack that targets the energy resources of WSNs. These attacks can be carried out in various ways, such as by sending malicious packets that cause the nodes to consume more energy than necessary or by exploiting network protocol vulnerabilities to disrupt the nodes' energy management mechanisms. This paper presents the Ensemble Learning Detection Model (ELDM), a novel approach to detecting and mitigating energy exhaustion attacks in WSNs. ELDM uses robust and reliable algorithms, including linear discriminant analysis (LDA), Subspace Discriminant Analysis (SDA), RUSBoosted Trees, AdaBoost Trees, Bagged Trees, and Subspace KNN. ELDM has a 99.7% accuracy rate and an effective Area Under the Curve value of 1.0, demonstrating its ability to efficiently detect different types of attacks, including new and unknown attacks. ELDM has the potential to significantly extend the lifetime of WSNs and ensure their uninterrupted operation in energy-constrained environments.

title: Verification of Resilient Current-State Opacity Against Dynamic Sensor Enabling Attacks
authors: Ziyue Ma, Yin Tong, Xiang Yin
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automatic Control
abstract: In this paper we study the problem of verifying resilient opacity in discrete-event systems in the presence of dynamic sensor attacks. The system contains several secret states, each assigned a secret level. The system is vulnerable to sensor enabling attacks by potential intruders: such attacks allow the intruder to make certain originally unobservable events temporarily observable. We propose the notion of resilient current-state opacity (R-CSO) which is a property that ensures an intruder cannot definitively determine that the system is currently in a secret state with a secret level $k$ by performing fewer than $k$ attacks. To verify R-CSO in a given system, we propose a novel type of bipartite transition system (BTS), called the R-CSO BTS, which models the interaction between the system and the intruder. Based on R-CSO BTS, we then derive a necessary and sufficient condition for verifying R-CSO. Finally, we prove that if the sensor mask is distinct, i.e., the intruder has the capability to differentiate between originally observable and attackable events, then the R-CSO verification can be simplified by only encodes binary attack decisions into the R-CSO BTS.

title: Relevance Structures are a Necessary Prerequisite for Artificial Metacognition
authors: Alexander M. Berenbeim, Ramneet Kaur, Adam Cobb, Brian Matejek, Anirban Roy, Susmit Jha, Nathaniel D. Bastian
publish: IEEE Intelligent Systems
abstract: AI-enabled systems deployed in dynamic, open-world, safety- and mission-critical environments often lack mechanisms for reliable self-monitoring and self-regulation under shift and perturbation. Artificial Metacognition (AM) addresses this by enabling systems to assess and adapt their own inference and learning processes. We show that relevance structures, which are order-theoretic substrates for comparing generalization and specialization and for quantifying under-coverage versus overreach, are structurally unavoidable for computable self-regulation. In turn, relevance structures provide an operational control theory for metacognitive tasks. We introduce exploratory, parsimonious, and balanced relevance scores and embed them into version-space algebra to regulate hypothesis search and inductive strategy selection. The resulting framework yields competence-aware learning systems that can both correct and explain their behavior by explicitly balancing exploration and parsimony. We illustrate relevance structures and their application with a medical visual question answering study on the SLAKE dataset.

title: On Maximum Correntropy GM-PHD Filtering
authors: Lin Gao, Jingling Li, Chaoqun Yang, Yao Zhou, Guobing Qian
publish: IEEE Signal Processing Letters
abstract: Multi-target tracking (MTT) in real-world environments often faces the challenge of outlier measurements, which severely degrades the performance of standard MTT algorithms. This paper integrates the maximum correntropy criterion (MCC) into the Gaussian mixture probability hypothesis density (GM-PHD) filter, an implicit data association and a highly efficient MTT algorithm. The MCC provides a localized similarity measure that is inherently resilient to impulsive outliers. We embed an iterative fixed-point measurement update for the GM-PHD filter, and an adaptive kernel size design strategy is also devised. The proposed MCC-GM-PHD filter effectively suppresses the influence of large measurement residuals, while maintaining a closed-form Gaussian mixture representation. The performance of the proposed MCC-GM-PHD filter is verified via simulations.

title: Impact of Electromagnetic Interference From Consumer Electronics and Bluetooth Crowding on GNSS-Based Performance in Mobile Devices
authors: Luca Morichi, Iman Ebrahimi Mehr, Mikhael Russo, Cristian Carallo, Riccardo Tommasi, Andrea Nardin
publish: IEEE Journal of Indoor and Seamless Positioning and Navigation
abstract: This paper explores the impact of electromagnetic interference (EMI) from consumer electronics and common household appliance on the performance of GNSS receivers in smartphones. As GNSS signals are weak and susceptible to external noise and interference, consumer appliances can introduce spurious emissions within GNSS frequency bands, leading to signal degradation. To investigate this, we conducted a series of controlled experiments simulating typical smartphone usage in proximity to different sources of EMI among household appliances (i.e., a microwave oven) and consumer electronics (i.e., Wi-Fi and Bluetooth devices), ultimately focusing on a dense Bluetooth environment. Our analysis reveals that the microwave oven can induce severe GNSS signal degradation, with substantial AGC and C/N<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$_{0}$</tex-math></inline-formula> drops (up to <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$2.3\,$</tex-math></inline-formula>dB-Hz) and significant satellite availability loss (up to <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$18 \%$</tex-math></inline-formula>), particularly in close-range configurations, leading to positioning RMSE increases exceeding <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$7\,$</tex-math></inline-formula> m. Additionally, Wi-Fi interference resulted in moderate reductions in signal quality, with positioning RMSE increases of less than <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$2\,$</tex-math></inline-formula> m in most scenarios. In contrast, Bluetooth exhibited negligible effects under tested conditions. These findings suggest potential implications for indoor navigation applications and underscore the importance of further improvements in interference mitigation for consumer GNSS devices.

title: Control-Aware Two-Stage Collision Screening for Real-Time Path Planning via MPC-Based Pose Correction and Certified Tube Inflation
authors: Jonghyup Lee
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Local path planning for autonomous driving often generates multiple candidates and selects a collision-free one based on geometric clearance over a finite prediction horizon. However, such geometric screening implicitly assumes ideal execution, and the resulting decision can be inconsistent with real closed-loop behavior when tracking deviations and model–plant mismatch occur. This paper proposes a control-aware planning framework that incorporates the closed-loop tracking envelope of a tube-based MPC tracker into collision decision making. At each step, the planner generates kinematically feasible quintic-polynomial candidates from an anchor on the previously selected path and discretizes them on the same prediction grid used by the controller. To enable real-time operation, we adopt a two-stage screening architecture. Stage 1 performs fast geometric gating using a bounding-box-based collision check and forwards a reduced set of promising candidates to the next stage using a lightweight proxy cost that promotes goal compliance and temporal consistency. Stage 2 then performs control-aware verification for each remaining candidate by solving a nominal tracking MPC with the candidate as the reference, using the candidate-conditioned predicted lateral deviation to shift the ego footprint toward the expected trajectory center (pose correction), and inflating the footprint by a certified, speed-parametric tube radius derived from the robust invariant set underlying tube MPC to cover bounded residual effects. The resulting shift-and-inflate screening rejects candidates that would collide under realistic closed-loop deviations while avoiding overly conservative symmetric margins. High-fidelity CarSim/Simulink simulations with static obstacles and dynamic cut-in scenarios demonstrate that the proposed method improves closed-loop safety and robustness compared to purely geometric screening and static-margin baselines under aggressive, high-speed maneuvers.

title: An Enhanced High-Gain Cubic Step-Up DC-DC Converter With Low Voltage Stress and Continuous Input Current for DC Microgrid Applications
authors: Mahendra Chand Bade, Sankar Peddapati, S. V. K. Naresh, Rambabu Kallelapu
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: In this work, a cubic step-up DC-DC converter (C<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sup>SUC) is configured in three stages to achieve high gain with reduced voltage stress on the switching elements. Also, the proposed topology highlights the feature of providing continuous input current and establishing a common ground between the input and output. Additionally, the proposed work offers an extremely high voltage gain while enabling the switches to operate at a lower duty ratio, resulting in improved efficiency. A detailed comparative description of works related to the state-of-the-art of high-gain topologies is presented. Moreover, the operating modes, small-signal modeling for stability, practical voltage gain, and efficiency as a function of duty cycle and various power levels of the proposed converter are presented. Further, a hardware prototype with a 300 W power rating and a 400 V output voltage has been implemented in the laboratory. The results obtained are in line with the theoretical findings, exhibiting a peak efficiency of 96.2% and a voltage stress of switching devices lower than the output voltage. For validation, the microcontroller TMS320f28379D is used to test the converter in both open-loop and closed-loop modes, yielding satisfactory results under the dynamic changes applied to the proposed cubic converter.

title:
Environmentally Responsive Ag/BaTiO
                    <sub>3</sub>
                    /TiO
                    <sub>2</sub>
                    Nanocomposite for Selective Gas Sensing and Advanced Piezo-Photocatalysis
authors: Lubna Aamir, Dipanjan Paul, Hissah Saedoon Albaqawi, Umer Farooq, Ghazala Yunus, R. K. Pandey, Deepshikha Rathore
publish: IEEE Sensors Journal
abstract: This article reports the environmental sensitivity including piezophotocatalytic activity and gas sensitivity of the Ag/BaTiO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sub>/TiO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> nanocomposite toward ethanol, toluene, and isopropanol. The structural properties and crystalline morphology of Ag/BaTiO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sub>/TiO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> nanocomposite were determined by analysing peak positions of XRD pattern, high-resolution TEM images including 2D faceted, alongside Moiré fringes, and SAED patterns. In gas sensing trials, it showcased n-type semiconductor behaviour, with rapid response times of 3-5 s and recovery times of 3-7 s toward each test gas, showing enhanced sensitivity with increasing flow rate and temperature with best response for ethanol. Further, 70% degradation of ARS dye was achieved in photocatalytic activity, which was enhanced to 90% via piezo-photocatalytic activity with Ag/BaTiO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sub>/TiO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> nanocomposite as catalyst. Consequently, these results present a promising new avenue for the development of advanced, efficient gas sensors and catalysts.

title: MuDPK: Knowledge Distillation with Multi-Granularity and Dynamic Priors for Graph Classification
authors: Baohang Wei, Ying Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Knowledge and Data Engineering
abstract: Knowledge distillation technology shows significant potential in transferring the knowledge of graph neural network (GNN) teacher models to multi-layer perceptron (MLP) student models. However, the existing methods are primarily designed for node classification task and are difficult to directly apply to graph classification. Graph classification task requires knowledge transfer via the generation of graph-level soft labels. Such global supervisory signals are inherently sparse, making it difficult for the student model to capture fine-grained structural information. Additionally, the student MLP model lacks the explicit ability to handle graph structures and has a significantly different representation space from the teacher GNN which further complicates the knowledge transfer process. We address these problems by proposing multigranularity distillation with a dynamic prior knowledge framework named MuDPK. The proposed framework integrates multigranularity structural information with the dynamic prior knowledge mechanism to effectively address the challenges of sparse supervision signals and the representation bottleneck in graph classification. We first design an adaptive graph propagation-based node feature enhancement module to enhance the representation capability of the student model in a limited feature space. We then design a triple distillation loss function that includes graph-level, subgraph-level, and node-level components. We alleviate the problem of sparse supervision signals by hierarchically aligning the structural representations of the teacher-student model at different levels. Finally, we use the intermediate layer features of the teacher as both the target representation and input knowledge, dynamically adjusted by our prior knowledge mechanism. Knowledge transfer is achieved through feature alignment while the student model is regulated by adjusting the proportion of injected knowledge in a controlled manner. Extensive experiments on eight benchmark graph datasets demonstrate the effectiveness and superiority of MuDPK, which achieves an average accuracy gain of +1.37% over the baselines.

title: Robust Fault Estimation for Linear Neutral Time-Delay Systems
authors: Nidhal Khorchani, Benjemaa Rabeb, Hassen Dahman, Mohamed Naceur Abdelkrim
publish: 2025 IEEE International Multi-Conference on Smart Systems &amp; Green Process (IMC-SSGP)
abstract: This study investigates the problem of robust fault detection (RFD) for a class of neutral systems subject to state delays and unknown inputs. The goal is to minimize the discrepancy between the generated residual signal and the actual fault, while improving robustness against both unknown and control inputs. The proposed approach frames the design of the robust fault detection filter (RFDF) as an <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$H_{\infty}$</tex> filtering problem. Sufficient conditions for the existence of such a filter are derived using linear matrix inequalities (LMIs). The effectiveness of the method is demonstrated through a numerical example.

title: Relative Navigation and Tracking Correction for Underwater Vehicle Swarms Using Dead Reckoning
authors: Maksymilian Wrzesień, Leszek Pietrukaniec
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer, Data Sciences and Applications (ACDSA)
abstract: This paper presents a relative navigation and tracking system for autonomous underwater vehicles (AUVs) operating in a swarm according to a leader-follower approach. The proposed system utilizes a Forward-Looking Sonar (FLS) for relative position estimation of a leader vehicle, combined with dead-reckoning (DR)-based correction to mitigate tracking errors caused by sonar scan delays and environmental disturbances. The leader AUV is equipped with high-precision navigation sensors (DVL, high-grade IMU), while follower vehicles rely on low-cost inertial sensors and mechanically scanning sonar. The system is implemented within the MOOS-IvP framework and validated through real-world experiments conducted at Lake Kosobudno, Poland. Results demonstrate that the integration of sonar-based tracking with dead-reckoning correction significantly improves the robustness and accuracy of relative positioning, ensuring formation stability even under conditions of intermittent sonar contact.

title: Cover Page
authors: N/A
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer, Data Sciences and Applications (ACDSA)

title: Meta-Learning for Underwater Diver Gesture Classification
authors: Stanisław Hożyń, Przemysław Karski
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer, Data Sciences and Applications (ACDSA)
abstract: With the advancement of underwater robotics, reliable diver-robot communication has become increasingly important. Since electromagnetic communication is strongly attenuated underwater, a practical alternative is a computer vision system that recognises diver gestures. In this work, we prepare and evaluate a meta-learning based training pipeline for underwater gesture recognition on a curated subset of the CADDY dataset. We compare three learning procedures using a shared Swin Transformer backbone: (i) supervised learning, (ii) supervised contrastive learning with a nearest-class-mean classifier, and (iii) few-shot learning with prototypical networks trained episodically. To support gesture-vocabulary design, we additionally propose a class-similarity analysis based on cosine similarities in the learned embedding space, summarised by heat maps and nearestneighbour confusion groups. The results show that episodic few-shot training provides the highest robustness, reaching an accuracy of 81.92% in the 5-shot setting, while the similarity analysis identifies gesture pairs that are visually close and therefore prone to confusion. Future work will focus on profiling inference latency and deploying the proposed approach in realtime underwater operational scenarios.

title: An Active Ankle–Foot Orthosis with IMU-Based Gait Detection for Foot Drop Assistance
authors: Theeraphong Wongrataphisan, Navapon Puttachai, Intarat Suksri, Sorrawit Tipwiang, Paphop Suwannaprom, Thanakarn Wannamai, Sari Manomaiphibul
publish: 2025 18th International Convention on Rehabilitation Engineering and Assistive Technology (i-CREATe)
abstract: This paper presents a lightweight and compact one-degree-of-freedom (1-DOF) active ankle-foot orthosis (AFO) designed to assist sagittal-plane motion in individuals with foot drop. The system delivers dorsiflexion support during swing and controlled plantarflexion during stance release using a 25 W backdrivable actuator capable of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.9 ~\mathrm{N} \cdot ~\mathrm{m}$</tex> output torque. The entire prototype weighs about 960 g and operates for approximately two hours on a 1600 mAh battery. A key innovation lies in its minimal-sensor, IMU-only architecture, employing a single shank-mounted inertial measurement unit (IMU) for gait-phase detection and control, thereby eliminating additional foot sensors and complex calibration. Shank orientation is estimated using the Madgwick filter, and heel-strike and toe-off events are detected from zero-crossings of shank angular velocity within a finite-state controller. Validation with a public gait dataset of healthy individuals (0.7-2.0 m/s) achieved sub-10 ms event detection accuracy, confirming the feasibility of reliable real-time gait detection using a single IMU. These results demonstrate a practical, low-cost solution with strong potential for future adaptive, user-specific gait assistance.

title: Grey Wolf Optimization for Service Life Maximization of Natural Fiber-Reinforced Transtibial Prosthetic Sockets
authors: Brahim Ben Smida, Wahbi Mankai, Moez Chafra, Ridha Ben Cheikh
publish: 2025 IEEE International Multi-Conference on Smart Systems &amp; Green Process (IMC-SSGP)
abstract: This paper presents a data-driven design for transtibial prosthetic sockets fabricated from natural fiberreinforced composites, aiming to extend their service life. An analytical model was established using experimental data from real medical prostheses to accurately capture the interplay between material properties, user activity profiles, and socket degradation mechanisms. The design parameters, identified through sensitivity analysis as most influential on the end-of-life behavior, were modeled under inherent uncertainties and constrained within practical upper and lower bounds. The optimization objective was defined as maximizing the service life of the prosthetic socket. To address this challenge, the Grey Wolf Optimization (GWO) algorithm was applied, owing to its recognized efficiency and strong convergence characteristics in solving complex benchmark problems. The results confirm that GWO provides rapid convergence and identifies optimal parameter sets within the defined search space, leading to improved life-cycle predictions. These findings demonstrate the relevance of nature-inspired metaheuristics in prosthetic design and offer a promising pathway for developing sustainable and high-performance socket solutions. The study establishes a preliminary framework that will be expanded in ongoing work to incorporate more advanced uncertainty quantification.

title: Cover Page
authors: N/A
publish: 2025 II International Congress of Engineering with a Social Sense (CIISS)

title: Biomedical Engineering: Designing Systems That Cooperate with Biological Functions
authors: Shigehiro Hashimoto
publish: 2025 18th International Convention on Rehabilitation Engineering and Assistive Technology (i-CREATe)
abstract: Biomedical engineering analyzes living organisms from an engineering perspective and explores the challenges of collaboration between living organisms and engineering technology. Biomedical engineering is a field that combines electrical and electronic engineering, mechanical engineering, materials science, informatics, medicine, and biology. Biomedical engineering focuses on various levels of living organisms, including the individual, organ, tissue, cell, and molecule. This article provides examples, focusing on the response of cells to mechanical and electrical stimuli. Biomedical engineering research elucidates the response of living organisms to external stimuli, and the results are useful for designing technologies and systems that work in harmony with biological functions in diagnosis and treatment based on the measurement and control of cells and tissues.

title: Explainable Reinforcement-Inspired Transformer Model for EEG Signal Denoising
authors: KGK Abeywardhane, R M B P M Rathnayake, KG Hasara Abeywardhane
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer, Data Sciences and Applications (ACDSA)
abstract: Electroencephalography (EEG) remains the most fundamental tool for identifying neurological and psychiatric disorders. However, the quality of EEG data is often degrated by unwanted artifacts that disort true brain activity, limiting the reliability of subsequent analysis. In view of the estimated 1.1 billion individuals worldwide who are living with mental health disorders [1] and [2], this showcases the urgent need for efficient, trustworthy, and interpretable EEG preprocessing methodologies. Traditional deep models often act as black boxes, preventing clinicians' confidence in outputs. In this work, we propose a Convolutional Neural Network Transformer Reinforcement Learning (CNN-Transformer-RL) with an Integrated Explainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI) framework that unites convolutional and transformer architectures, RL-style adaptive fine-tuning, and XAI via Integrated Gradients (IG) and attention. The model is pretrained with Mean Squared Error (MSE) loss and then fine-tuned using an XAI-weighted loss that encourages retention of high-attribution temporal features. In demonstration of both synthetic and real EEG datasets, the proposed framework achieved higher denoising accuracy with MSE <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$=0.009$</tex>, Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$=0.095$</tex>, Mean Absolute Error (MAE) <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$=0.071$</tex>. Person <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{R} \boldsymbol{=} \mathbf{0. 6 3}$</tex>, indicating its denoising precision and reliable correlation retention. Model interpretability was validated through attention heatmaps and IG analyses, which focus highly on physiologically relevant temporal patterns. This integration of transparency and adaptive optimization within training is used to increase the interpretability and preclinical applicability of the denoising framework. To strengthen practical relevance, the study investigates <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\alpha$</tex> sweep ablations, baseline evaluations, and cross-dataset validation supporting future realworld applications.

title: Temporal Simulation of Tumor Progression in Breast Imaging Using Conditional GANs
authors: Vishwaganesan B S, Kaja Mohideen A
publish: 2026 International Conference on Visual Analytics and Data Visualization (ICVADV)
abstract: The paper introduces a framework of the temporal simulation of tumor progression in mammographic imaging through a Conditional Generative Adversarial Network (CGAN). The lack of publicly provided longitudinal mammography datasets makes the proposed method use a weakly supervised approach by building pseudo-temporal steps of progression using the morphological features of lesions. The datasets of the MIAS and CBIS-DDSM mammograms are used to create paired samples of images and masks and extract stage labels according to lesion size and shape properties. CGAN is trained with these labels and learns to produce artificial sequential measures of tumor growth to produce clinically plausible patterns of lesion evolution. The consistency of quantitative structures and visual qualitative analyses show the consistency and realism of the created temporal structures. The suggested framework will be an effective way to cope with the lack of longitudinal data regarding breast imaging and create powerful artificial intelligence tools to examine disease progression.

title: Optimizing Machine Learning Inference Serving Systems: A Survey
authors: Shirin Ebadi, Eric Keller
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer, Data Sciences and Applications (ACDSA)
abstract: For a long time, Machine Learning (ML) training has been the primary focus of both academia and industry, while inference was often treated as a simpler process that developers manually assemble its components. However, the demand for inference services has been rapidly increasing, with ML inference consuming more than 90% of infrastructure costs at Amazon Web Services. This makes the development of efficient inference systems increasingly important. The performance of these services, beyond model-level optimizations, is highly dependent on system-level optimizations, where various trade-offs arise. This survey examines techniques for resource usage optimization and latency improvement in ML inference serving within server-based infrastructures, focusing on research contributions from the last three years. We explore key optimization areas, including resource scheduling and autoscaling methods, batching and throughput improvement techniques, multi-tenancy and GPU sharing strategies that mitigate interference, latency-accuracy trade-off mechanisms such as model selection, fast-path optimizations and early-exit strategies, as well as emerging paradigms like programmable data planes and serverless computing. By summarizing recent works from top venues and highlighting open challenges, we aim to provide a comprehensive overview of the latest advances, opportunities, and future research directions in ML inference serving system optimizations.

title: Enhancing Academic Support with AI: A Case Study of Chatbot Integration Using Microsoft Copilot Studio at Jazan University
authors: Shakeel Ahmed, Reema Shaheen, Ahmed Sawy Khaled, Muhammad Imran Satti, Syed Hassan Raza, Muhammad Shahid Dildar, Muhammad Usama Shakeel
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer, Data Sciences and Applications (ACDSA)
abstract: Higher education is rapidly changing with the help of real-time academic support from artificial intelligence (AI). In this research, Jizan University uses Microsoft Copilot Studio to design and implement an intelligent chatbot. The designed system is serving both the university website and Blackboard LMS. The AI-powered chatbot automates responses to support students' engagement and frequently asked questions, reducing the administration's workload. The Formative Research involved 90 participants, including faculty and students, who reported that the chatbot considerably lessens the response time as compared to the personalized support. Satisfaction rate is over 70 %, and response time is less than three seconds. The designed system uncovered areas for enhancement in managing complex or multilingual and academic inquiries. The chatbot's user-friendly no-code framework, transparent integration, and event-based logic employing Microsoft Power Automate highlights its scalability and Versatility. This research highlights the role of AI agents in authenticating Web-Based Service Delivery in Saudi Arabia's institutions by using Copilot Studio as an accessible development platform.

title: The Impact of Sojourn Time Distributions on the Price of Anarchy
authors: Yunpeng Li, Antonis Dimakis, Yingjun Shen, Qisheng Huang, Costas Courcoubetis
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer, Data Sciences and Applications (ACDSA)
abstract: Mean Field Games (MFGs) provide a powerful framework for modeling strategic interactions in large-scale multi-agent systems. This paper studies continuous-time MFGs with finite action spaces and general sojourn-time distributions, extending beyond the classical exponential assumption. We establish the existence of stationary equilibria under mild regularity conditions and show that stateless continuous-time MFGs are equivalent to population games. Our main contribution is a systematic efficiency analysis via the Price of Anarchy (PoA) that highlights the critical role of sojourn-time distributions. We characterize extremal PoA values across different distributional families and identify distributions that achieve them. In particular, we prove a uniform lower bound of two on the PoA that holds across all feasible families of sojourn-time distributions satisfying a mild mean-richness condition. This result shows that inefficiency is intrinsic to decentralized decision-making and cannot be eliminated by distributional modeling choices alone. Together, our findings demonstrate that distributional heterogeneity in delays is a first-order determinant of efficiency in continuous-time multi-agent systems.

title: EDGE-ASSISTED COOPERATIVE PERCEPTION IN VEHICULAR NETWORKS: Proposing a New System
authors: Ruiqi Wang, Guohong Cao
publish: IEEE Vehicular Technology Magazine
abstract: Autonomous driving and advanced driver assistance systems (ADASs) have rapidly advanced in recent years. These systems rely on various sensors, such as cameras and depth sensors, to perceive their surroundings and apply artificial intelligence (AI) techniques to understand traffic conditions and enhance safety. However, these sensors are inherently limited by line of sight, making it difficult to detect objects behind occlusions that can pose serious safety risks. To overcome this limitation, multiple vehicles can collaboratively share their perception data via an edge server. Although this cooperative approach enhances situational awareness, it introduces several technical challenges, including handling large volumes of data, high computational demands, and constrained wireless bandwidth, especially in dense urban traffic. In this article, we present a cooperative perception system that enhances driving safety by efficiently collecting, processing, and disseminating perception data in real time. Our system features a depth-based region of interest (RoI) extraction technique to reduce upload bandwidth and a relevance-aware dissemination algorithm to minimize unnecessary data delivery. Together, these components enable scalable and resource-efficient perception sharing, making cooperative perception practical for real-world vehicular environments.

title: Improving Thermal Comfort by Integrating Phase Change Materials into a Hollow Brick: Numerical Study
authors: Hamden Jawaher, Kria Fatma, Chtourou Sirine, Hammami Moez, Baccar Mounir
publish: 2025 IEEE International Multi-Conference on Smart Systems &amp; Green Process (IMC-SSGP)
abstract: This paper presents a numerical study of the thermal behavior of RT25 phase change material (PCM) embedded in the cavities of hollow bricks. The objective is to evaluate the effect of this configuration on the regulation of indoor temperature in buildings exposed to summer conditions. The transient analysis was performed using ANSYS Fluent R19, based on the finite element method (FEM).
