The research data were obtained from the annual financial reports of ten manufacturing companies in the plastics and packaging sub-sector listed on the Indonesia Stock Exchange for the period 2022-2024, with input variables in the form of Gross Profit Margin (GPM), Net Profit Margin (NPM), Return on Assets (ROA), and Return on Equity (ROE). Company performance is classified into two categories, namely Healthy and Unhealthy. This study compares three machine learning algorithms, namely Decision Tree, Support Vector Machine (SVM), and Artificial Neural Network (ANN), with a focus on hyperparameter optimization using GridSearchCV and performance testing through K-Fold Cross Validation. Model evaluation was conducted using several classification metrics, including Accuracy, F1-score, ROC Curve, AUC, and Confusion Matrix. The results show that the proposed machine learning approach yields high classification performance in assessing the financial health of manufacturing companies on the observed dataset, with the Decision Tree emerging as the most consistent model.

title: Preliminary Computational Analysis of the Lignin Peroxidase H8 Isozyme Structure of Phanerochaete Chrysosporium Reveals Instability in Disulfide Bonds in the Wild-Type Tertiary Conformation
authors: Uvin Eksith Senadheera, Yuya Takimoto, Tadachika Nakayama, Jasintha Jayasanka
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer, Data Sciences and Applications (ACDSA)
abstract: Lignin peroxidase H8 is a polymorphic enzyme found in the fungus Phanerochaete chrysosporium. Among the isozymes of lignin peroxidase in P. chrysosporium, LiPH8is of biotechnological appeal due to its promising catalytic activity in cleaving C-C bonds and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\beta-\mathrm{O}-4^{\prime}$</tex> aryl ether bonds at high efficiency and its ability to perform under acidic microenvironments. The study utilized computational biology approaches in investigating the intricate tertiary structure of Lignin peroxidase H8 to gain insights into its thermodynamic and physio-chemical properties. Amino acid sequences were retrieved from UniProt, and the homology models were built with ProMod3 and validated. Physico-chemical and thermodynamic properties of the structure were computed using ProtParam. The study indicates that the free energy due to disulfide interactions in the isozyme structure is computed to be 18.4 Kcal/mol, which is unfavourable for protein stabilization strain in the disulfide bonds, which may destabilize the tertiary structure. This strain could lead to reduced conformational flexibility and potentially impair catalytic efficiency under acidic conditions, indicating potential instability due to disulfide bonds. Disulfide substitution studies are recommended to reduce strain and improve stability. Aliphatic index, isoelectric point, and Grand average hydropathicity indicate that the isozyme is suitable for enzymatic treatments. The instability index of 40.6 suggests that the isozyme is slightly unstable, indicating that the wild-type isozyme may require pretreatment modification in in-vitro biotechnological use. The half-life of the isozyme is relatively shorter, with 40 to 30 hours in mammalian reticulocytes, <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$>20$</tex> hours in Yeast, and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$>10$</tex> hours in Escherichia coli. This suggests that the enzyme is less stable within simpler organisms, suggesting that the improved versions of the wild-type isozymes might be effective in bioengineered eukaryotic host environments.

title: Index
authors: N/A
publish: 2025 Asia Pacific Workshop on Data Science and Information Theory (APWDSIT)

title: UV-Vis Spectroscopic Analysis of Gold Nanoparticles: Unveiling the Impact of Synthesis Parameters on Absorbance and FWHM
authors: Anis Diyana Rosli, Nor Shahanim Mohamad Hadis, Khairul Adib Ishak, Aida Zulia Zulhanip, Nur Atiqah Azman, Norazwana Mohd Najib
publish: 2025 5th International Symposium on Materials and Electrical Engineering (ISMEE)
abstract: Gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) exhibit unique optical properties that are highly sensitive to synthesis conditions, making them valuable in sensing, imaging, and therapeutic applications. Although the citrate reduction method is well established, the specific effects of temperature and citrate-to-gold molar ratio on the optical properties of AuNPs particularly absorbance intensity, surface plasmon resonance (SPR) peak position, and full width at half maximum (FWHM) are not yet fully understood. This study investigates these effects using UV-Vis spectroscopy, focusing on synthesis temperatures between 90-100°C and citrate-to-gold molar ratios ranging from 2.3 to 3.0. Samples with lower molar ratios (2.3 and 2.5) exhibited significantly higher absorbance values (approximately 4-7) and longer wavelength SPR peaks (528-534 nm), while samples with higher molar ratios (2.8 and 3.0) showed markedly lower absorbance values (below 1) and shorter wavelength SPR peaks (519-526 nm). In contrast, the effect of temperature on absorbance intensity was subtle. Analysis of FWHM values indicated that molar ratio also significantly influenced particle homogeneity in the lower ratios (2.3 and 2.5), with samples at a molar ratio of 2.5 demonstrating superior homogeneity with the narrowest FWHM (1 0. 0 0 - 3 2. 2 6 nm). The impact of temperature on FWHM was most notable at the lowest molar ratio (2.3), where higher temperatures led to increased heterogeneity. Based on literature comparisons of SPR peak positions, the estimated size range for synthesized AuNPs was approximately 10-74 nm. This work highlights the significant influence of both citrate-to-gold molar ratio and temperature on the optical properties of AuNPs and provides a foundation for optimizing synthesis protocols for targeted applications.

title: Effectiveness of Slow-Paced Serious Puzzle Game on Generalized Anxiety Disorder: Heart Rate Monitoring Biofeedback and User Experience
authors: Justin Tjokro, Dimas Ramdhan, David Drago, George Mercedes Junior Suitela
publish: 2026 International Seminar on Intelligent Business and Edge-Computing Research (ISIBER)
abstract: Generalized Anxiety Disorder (GAD) is a common mental health condition that affects emotional regulation and daily lives. This study examines the effectiveness of Little Space Mechanic, a slow paced serious puzzle game as a supportive intervention for individuals with GAD in the form of problem solving therapy. Thirty participants were classified into groups based on their level of anxiety score obtained from the GAD-7 questionnaire and completed a gameplay session while their heart rate was monitored using a smartwatch. Upon finishing the game, their user experience was assessed through Game Experience Questionnaire (GEQ). Results show that the moderate/sever anxiety group exhibited significantly higher heart rates during gameplay, though values remained stable within normal physiological limits. GEQ findings reflect strong scores in competence, immersion, and positive affect. These results suggest that Little Space Mechanic provides a calming, engaging experience and demonstrates potential as a serious game based PST for anxiety management.

title: Comparison of 3D Web Development Frameworks for Ground Motion Simulation in a Virtual 3D Environment
authors: Nyles Chan, Marnel Peradilla, Fritz Kevin Flores, Rafael Cabredo
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer, Data Sciences and Applications (ACDSA)
abstract: Ground motion studies can be performed in many different ways, but there are no studies of ground motion visualization using a 3D web framework. Therefore, five different 3D web development frameworks were assessed, with two frameworks - ThreeJS and BabylonJS - selected for further evaluation. A sample structure is rendered into each 3D web framework, where it is tested to withstand the intensity of ground motion via simulation, demonstrating the potential power of earthquakes and its impact on structures. Their differences in performance metrics are compared to each other through built-in functions in Javascript, to see which framework is more efficient for the purposes of ground motion simulation. The accuracy of the ground motion data translated into 3D simulations is also observed with the help of PEER Ground Motion Database and a shake table. The results show that BabylonJS is heavier than ThreeJS despite BabylonJS being more fit for the purposes of this study. However, both 3D web frameworks are able to mimic the benchmark data with accuracy.

title: Malicious Web Page Detection: A Comparative Analysis of Machine Learning with Geospatial Features
authors: M. Dja'far Karzein Hafidz Yuntanozra, Nur Aulia Pertiwi, Muhammad Faki Raihan, Friza Arya Wisono, Ilham Mulya Cahya Satria
publish: 2026 International Seminar on Intelligent Business and Edge-Computing Research (ISIBER)
abstract: Malicious web pages pose a persistent threat to cybersecurity, enabling data theft, phishing activity, and malware distribution. In this study, we conduct a comparative analysis of four classification models XGBoost, Logistic Regression, Decision Tree, and Deep Neural Network (DNN) to detect malicious and benign web pages on a large-scale dataset. The experiment uses a publicly available dataset containing more than 1.2 million URLs, where feature engineering is performed on both lexical attributes and geospatial characteristics following an extensive Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA). The models are evaluated using Accuracy, Precision, Recall, and F1-Score. The results show that XGBoost achieves the best overall performance, with an Accuracy of 99.96% and an F1-Score of 99.11%, while DNN attains the highest Recall (99.18%), indicating a stronger capability in identifying malicious cases and minimizing false negatives. In addition to providing a quantitative benchmark across multiple algorithms, this study highlights the predictive contribution of geospatial features such as server location and IP-based geographic distribution which remain underexplored in prior work, particularly in large-scale real-world datasets. The findings demonstrate that ensemble learning and deep learning approaches are highly effective for malicious web page detection and may support the development of more adaptive and datadriven cybersecurity systems.

title: An Edge AIoT Framework with Temporal Convolutional Networks for Site-Specific Fertilizer Recommendation: A Simulation Study using IoT-Based Field Data
authors: R. Hafid Hardyanto, Moh. Khairudin, Rustam Asnawi
publish: 2025 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Blockchain, Cloud Computing, and Data Analytics (ICoABCD)
abstract: Efficient and sustainable fertilizer management is critical for enhancing rice productivity while minimizing environmental degradation caused by excessive nitrogen, phosphorus, and potassium use. Conventional fertilization methods often rely on intuition and ignore sitespecific variability in soil, crops, and weather conditions. This paper presents an Edge Artificial Intelligence of Things (Edge AIoT) framework that employs a Temporal Convolutional Network (TCN) with attention for site-specific fertilizer recommendation in rice farming. The system integrates multimodal IoT sensor data-comprising soil moisture, temperature, humidity, rainfall, and light intensityprocessed in a simulated low-power edge environment (Raspberry Pi 4). The TCN, implemented with dilated causal convolutions and attention, captures both short-term fluctuations and long-term seasonal nutrient trends. The trained model was quantized into TensorFlow Lite (22.51 KB) for efficient on-device inference. Simulation experiments using field-acquired IoT sensor data demonstrate that the quantized TCN+Attention model achieves MAE <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$=0.025$</tex>, RMSE <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$=0.021$</tex>, and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{R}^{\mathbf{2}}=0.906$</tex>, confirming its suitability for real-time edge deployment. These findings highlight the feasibility of scalable, low-latency AIoT-based nutrient recommendation systems that can support sustainable fertilizer management in rural rice farming.

title: Reliability of Deep Neural Network Models in Large-Scale Federated Learning Implementations
authors: Yohannes Bekele, Mansi Bhavsar
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer, Data Sciences and Applications (ACDSA)
abstract: For mission- and safety-critical applications, ensuring the dependability of machine and deep learning models on edge devices is essential, particularly in environments where hardware failures are common. A fault-resilience benchmarking study of five representative models-Logistic Regression, Random Forest, CNN, LSTM, and MobileNet-under five fault types-single-bit, double-bit, zero-bit, least significant bit, and most significant bit-is presented in this paper. We simulate soft errors during federated learning using a modular application-level fault injection, and we assess their impact on model accuracy, loss, and Silent Data Corruption (SDC) rates on various datasets. The findings indicate that while LSTM and MobileNet are the most susceptible, CNNs have the highest fault tolerance. The most disruptive faults are zero-bit and MSB ones. We validate findings statistically and propose a reliability ranking across architectures. This work provides practical insights for selecting robust models in edge AI and highlights fault-aware model selection strategies for reliable Federated Learning (FL) in safety-critical systems.

title: Cybersecurity Awareness in Mobile Banking: Users' Perception of Risks and Data Protection in the Greater Jakarta Area
authors: Selly Angelina, Stevanno Verdianta, Dedy Syamsuar
publish: 2026 International Seminar on Intelligent Business and Edge-Computing Research (ISIBER)
abstract: Mobile banking has become a vital component of digital financial services, offering convenient and real-time access to various banking activities. However, its rapid adoption has not been matched by adequate cybersecurity awareness among users, particularly in developing economies such as Indonesia. This study examined the level of cybersecurity awareness among Gen Z and university student mobile banking users in the Greater Jakarta Area, a region with high digital penetration and financial technology usage. An extended Technology Acceptance Model (TAM) was employed by integrating additional constructs such as trust, perceived risk, self-efficacy, cybersecurity knowledge, and data protection awareness to analyze key behavioral drivers. Data were collected from more than 385 respondents through a structured online and offline questionnaire, and the results were analyzed using Partial Least Squares Structural Equation Modeling (PLS-SEM). The findings are expected to offer valuable insights for financial institutions, mobile banking providers, and policymakers in designing targeted, user-centric cybersecurity strategies that enhance safe digital behavior, strengthen user protection, and reduce overall vulnerability to cyber threats.

title: Automated Chemical Component Identification of Inorganic Salt Drying Patterns Using Deep Learning
authors: Srinivasa Kranthi Kiran Kolachina, Ruth Agada, Rim Hadidi, Jie Yan, Beni B. Dangi, Oliver Steinbock
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer, Data Sciences and Applications (ACDSA)
abstract: Understanding the drying dynamics of inorganic salt droplets is vital for advancing applications in materials science, pharmaceuticals, and environmental engineering. This study explores the use of hybrid deep learning models, specifically CNN-LSTM architectures, to classify three salts—ammonium chloride <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\left(\text{NH}_{4} \text{Cl}\right)$</tex>, potassium chloride <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(\text{KCl})$</tex>, and sodium chloride (NaCl)—based on their evaporation-induced crystallization behavior. High-speed video data (50 FPS) were captured under acoustic levitation, and the videos were segmented into 10-frame clips. To address significant class imbalance, a multifaceted approach including class weighting, oversampling, and data augmentation was implemented. Four models—VGG16-LSTM, DenseNet121-LSTM, ResNet18-LSTM, and a Vanilla CNN-LSTM—were trained and evaluated. The DenseNet121-LSTM model achieved the highest performance, with an overall accuracy of 80% and a macro F1-score of 0.82, demonstrating its ability to capture complex spatiotemporal features. These findings underscore the potential of deep learning for analyzing droplet crystallization patterns and highlight its broader applicability in automated process monitoring and scientific diagnostics.

title: Valorization of Reverse Osmosis Brine into Mg-Al LDH Adsorbents for Dye Wastewater Treatment
authors: Chrifa Guerfel, Khoulod Benmarzoug, Mariem Ahbil, Hedi Ben Amor, Margarita Vázquez-González, Miguel A. Correa-Duarte
publish: 2025 IEEE International Multi-Conference on Smart Systems &amp; Green Process (IMC-SSGP)
abstract: Brine generated from seawater desalination, with a salinity nearly twice that of natural seawater, poses significant environmental challenges. Nevertheless, its high salt content also makes it a valuable secondary resource for the synthesis of high-value materials. In this study, a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{Mg}-\text{Al}-\text{CO}_{3}$</tex> layered double hydroxide (LDH) was synthesized from reverse osmosis brine collected from the Djerba desalination plant in southern Tunisia. The calcined LDH (LDH-C) was tested as an adsorbent for the removal of Congo Red (CR) dye, an anionic dye, from synthetic aqueous solutions. Structural characterization by XRD and FTIR confirmed the successful synthesis of the LDH. Batch adsorption experiments revealed that CR uptake was slightly influenced by pH, but strongly by contact time. The adsorption process followed pseudo-first-order kinetic model, and the Langmuir isotherm provided the best fit for describing CR adsorption onto LDH-C showing a maximal adsorption capacity of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$290 \text{mg}. \mathrm{g}^{-1}$</tex>. Overall, these results highlight the potential of desalination brine as a sustainable precursor for developing efficient LDH-based adsorbents for dye removal from wastewater.

title: Numerical Investigation of Horizontal Fin Length Effect on the Thermal Performance of a Trombe Wall
authors: Khaoula Friji, Abdallah Bouabidi, Farhan Lafta Rashid, Ali M. Ashour
publish: 2025 IEEE International Multi-Conference on Smart Systems &amp; Green Process (IMC-SSGP)
abstract: This study investigates the thermal performance of a finned Trombe wall system through a validated 2D Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) model. The simulations were performed under steady-state conditions using the RNG k <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\varepsilon$</tex> turbulence model and DO radiation model, with solar heat flux applied on the external glazing and adiabatic lateral boundaries. The model showed good agreement with experimental measurements of temperature, pressure, and velocity fields. The novelty of this work lies in the parametric optimization of fin length (0.1-0.2 m) on the absorber surface, an approach rarely reported in Trombe wall studies. Results demonstrate that longer fins enhance airflow temperature by up to XX% and strengthen natural convection, leading to improved thermal efficiency of the passive solar heating system. These findings provide design insights for geometric optimization of Trombe walls to maximize solar heat utilization.

title: Deep Learning-Based Comparison of YOLOv8 and EfficientNetB0 for Plant Disease Detection and Classification
authors: Atitallah Fatma, Thabet Assem, Dominique MARTINEZ, Mohamed Boutayeb, Mohamed AOUN
publish: 2025 IEEE International Multi-Conference on Smart Systems &amp; Green Process (IMC-SSGP)
abstract: Timely and accurate identification of plant diseases is essential for enhancing crop productivity and promoting sustainable agriculture. In this study, we propose a deep learning-based framework for both classification and real-time detection of plant diseases, using high-resolution images from public datasets. The EfficientNetB0 model, fine-tuned via transfer learning, is evaluated on both the PlantVillage and PlantDoc datasets, with PlantVillage selected for its higher quality and diversity. For real-time detection, YOLOv8 was trained and tested on the PlantVillage dataset, demonstrating faster inference times while maintaining comparable detection accuracy compared to the EfficientNet model. Experimental results highlight the effectiveness of lightweight convolutional neural networks for deployment in precision agriculture, particularly in resource-constrained environments.

title: Developing a Participation Mapping Framework (PMF) for Ethical and Inclusive Smart City Governance
authors: Amin Khoshkenar, Mahnaz Hassanpour, Hala Nassereddine
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer, Data Sciences and Applications (ACDSA)
abstract: Accelerating digitalization has restructured urban governance, while smart city initiatives often reproduce inequalities and weaken citizen agency. In this research, the Participation Mapping Framework (PMF) has been developed which is a stage-based framework that integrates participation, ethics, and evaluation across the lifecycle of smart cities. The research is based on a theory-driven, systematic review of 27 peer-reviewed and policy sources published between 2020 and 2025, synthesizing worldwide initiatives and civic-technology practices. PMF maps participation through linking six stages of developing smart cities (visioning, planning, design, implementation, operation, and evaluation) to four main dimensions of participation (goals, modes and tools, metrics, and safeguards). The findings identify that enhanced legitimacy and trust can be achieved through early and continuous participation, and integrating ethical safeguards such as transparency, privacy, and algorithmic accountability transform participation into shared governance which enables citizens to act as co-creators of transparent, equitable, and trustworthy smart cities. PMF serves as a diagnostic tool for policymakers to benchmark participatory maturity. It also serves as an analytical lens used by researchers in order to examine the inclusiveness and the quality of the governance.

title: Towards Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINN) for EEG Source Localization: Finite Element Head Model Development and Data Generation
authors: Amjad Sultan Awan, Mary Baker
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer, Data Sciences and Applications (ACDSA)
abstract: Electroencephalography (EEG) source localization is an ill-posed inverse problem, traditionally addressed with either parametric dipole fitting or distributed inverse algorithms constrained by priors. Recently, data-driven methods have emerged, but they often rely on purely simulation-based training data without explicitly enforcing the physics of EEG signal propagation. In this work, we present a finite element model of the human head using a four-layer spherical volume conductor. This forward model, implemented in COMSOL Multiphysics, provides accurate simulations of scalp potentials generated by current dipole sources in the brain. We demonstrate that our model's output agrees with the analytical solution for the classical foursphere head model, ensuring its validity as a ground truth generator. The synthetic EEG data produced by this model can be used to train and validate a novel physics-informed neural network (PINN) for EEG source localization. This represents a first step towards integrating physical law constraints directly into deep learning for EEG source imaging.

title: Proactive Collision Avoidance and Traffic Management via Sensor Fusion and V2V Communication
authors: Vishal K, Lokesh V, Asha S
publish: 2026 International Conference on Visual Analytics and Data Visualization (ICVADV)
abstract: Road traffic accidents and congestion are primarily caused by delayed driver response and insufficient real-time awareness of surrounding vehicles. This paper presents a Proactive Collision Avoidance and Traffic Management System based on sensor fusion and vehicle-to-vehicle (V2V) communication, designed to predict and mitigate collision risks before they occur. The proposed framework integrates ultrasonic, infrared, and GPS sensors to estimate inter-vehicle distance and relative motion. At the same time, a 2.4 GHz RF communication module enables low-latency exchange of emergency alerts among nearby vehicles. A weighted sensor fusion model combined with real-time risk estimation allows early detection of hazardous scenarios and supports timely driver alerts or automated interventions. Experimental evaluation under simulated and controlled traffic conditions demonstrates an obstacle detection accuracy exceeding 95%, false-positive rates below 3%, and collision response times of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$150 - 200 \text{ms}$</tex>, significantly outperforming conventional single-sensor and human-reaction-based systems. The results confirm that integrating predictive sensing with cooperative V2V communication enhances traffic safety, reduces congestion, and provides a scalable foundation for intelligent transportation systems.

title: Integrated 4D Clinical Computational Taxonomy and Translation Roadmap for GAN-Based MRI Super-Resolution
authors: Satvik V Khara, Gaurav D Tivari, Vraj Thakkar, Prayas Chavda, Anurag Soliya, Nisarg Chauhan
publish: 2026 International Conference on Visual Analytics and Data Visualization (ICVADV)
abstract: Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) is a critical diagnostic modality because of its high soft-tissue contrast and non-invasive nature. However, long acquisition times and hardware limitations often yield images with suboptimal spatial resolution. Super-resolution (SR) methods aim to reconstruct high-resolution (HR) images from low-resolution (LR) inputs, thereby enhancing diagnostic quality without prolonging scan times. Among deep learning-based methods, Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) have been demonstrated to be better at recovering fine anatomical structures with realistic textures. This work reviews GAN-based super-resolution techniques in MRI and presents an integrated 4D ClinicalComputational Taxonomy (4D-CCT) for GAN-based MRI super resolution that categorizes the existing approaches based on architecture type, imaging modality, application domain, and training paradigm. This taxonomy is developed to address inconsistencies in the previous classifications and to enable structured comparison. In addition, the study outlines a fivephase clinical translation roadmap that bridges the gap between algorithmic advances and practical deployment. This review synthesizes key challenges, evaluation metrics and possible solutions; therefore, it serves as a reference for both researchers and clinicians interested in high-impact enhancement of MRI. By supporting faster, more accurate and more accessible medical imaging, this research work aligns with the health innovation objectives of the United Nations Sustainable Development Goal (SDG) 3 - Good Health and Well-Being, contributing toward improved diagnostic capability and healthcare outcomes.

title: Beer-Lambert Law-Based Optical Biosensor Simulation for PSA Detection Using 45mm CMOS Technology
authors: Ameur El Jeljal, Mounir Ouremchi, Karim El Khadiri, Moulhime El Bekkali
publish: 2025 International Conference on Advanced Technologies and Interdisciplinary Innovation (ICAT2I)
abstract: The creation and simulation of a photodetector sensor for the identification of PSA (Prostate-Specific Antigen), a key biomarker in the early diagnosis of prostate cancer, are described in this study. The goal is to create a full optoelectronic sensor based on the Beer-Lambert law that relates optical absorbance to PSA concentration. The sensor would integrate a CMOS photodiode modeled in Verilog-A with a high-gain transimpedance amplifier (TIA) built in 45 nm CMOS technology The model recreates the optical and enzymatic response produced by the HRP-TMB reacting thereby demonstrating the link between the concentration of PSA and the generated photocurrent. Simulations conducted using Cadence Virtuoso show that, as the PSA concentration increases from 0 to 50 ng/mL, the photodiode current decreases from 410 nA to 80 nA and the output voltage Vout changes from 928 mV to 813 mV. The results are consistent with the experimental studies [4] and [5]. With a gain of around 127 dB <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\Omega$</tex>, the integrated TIA, which was modeled after the [1] piece provides reliable and low-noise operation. It should be emphasized that this research is completely simulation-based and does not include any actual hardware measurements or experimental verification. This approach forms a strong foundation for the implementation of CMOS multi-sensor biochips designed for optical bio identification.

title: A Review of AI Application in Circular Economy for Sustainability in the Energy Sector
authors: Paula Marielle Ababao, Gabrielle George G. Banaag, Ryan Phillip C. Co, George Andrew P. Untalan, Jackie Lou O. Raborar
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer, Data Sciences and Applications (ACDSA)
abstract: Amid escalating climate challenges and the inefficiencies of linear economic models, this review examines how artificial intelligence (AI) can enable circular economy (CE) strategies to advance environmental sustainability in the energy sector. Drawing on 40 peer-reviewed studies from 2020 to 2025, the research highlights AI applications like machine learning, predictive analytics, and digital twins which optimize resource use, reduce waste, and enhance energy efficiency across industrial and energy systems. AI demonstrates significant potential in integrating renewables, extending battery life, optimizing smart grids, and transforming market operations through automated trading and blockchain-enabled transparency. However, barriers like high costs, data accessibility issues, regulatory gaps, and skills shortages hinder widespread adoption, particularly among small and medium enterprises. Emerging solutions, including federated learning for decentralized energy management and simulationbased design for material efficiency, offer promising pathways forward. The study emphasizes the need for targeted policies, open data frameworks, and interdisciplinary collaboration to address systemic challenges. By providing a comprehensive review that aims to accelerate the integration of CE principles into the energy sector through AI, while calling for further empirical research to evaluate long-term sustainability outcomes and ethical implications.

title: Comparative Study on the Transition from Neural Networks to Erudition Automata
authors: Shree Smeka J, V Sheeja Kumari, K. Sabarigirivason, Arvind Kumar Srivastava, S. P. Santhoshkumar, Prince Arora
publish: 2025 International Conference on Emerging Technologies in Electronics and Green Energy (ICETEG)
abstract: This research considers the evolution of Erudition Automata (EA) from Neural Network. Artificial Intelligence has entirely evolved because of the adoption of Neural Networks, especially in the areas of recognizing patterns and learning new things. While these constructs of their functionality do offer some unprecedented benefits, their excess reliance on data, increased inference, and training time, and poor explainability makes deployment and usage of AI systems difficult and opaque. The focus of Erudition Automata is to resolve the inadequacies posed by Neural Networks, by enhanced integration of automaton theory and cognitive processes, including structured rule-based reasoning, and modularity. This research presents a comparative study of the structure and application of EA's and neural networks. Currently, neural networks are best for performing image recognition and video processing whereas EAs can be used in development of decision support and real time analytics tools as these systems are more adept to work in environments requiring logic and well thought out reasoning. To that end, each paradigm section addresses the performance issues of computational efficacy, scalability, and reliability. The results indicate that when greater transparency and order are needed, it is worthwhile to consider EAs. Some recommendations for the future are to couple the capacity of neural network system with the reasoning capacity of EAs. This research paper points that ramifying <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$A I$</tex> architectures with the help of Erudition Automata is an exciting future perspective for development of the field with regard the balance between interpretability and computational complexity.

title: EEG-Based Emotion Classification Using Wavelet Features Extraction and a Hybrid CNN-Mamba
authors: Pandu Setia Anugrah, Esmeralda Contessa Djamal
publish: 2026 International Seminar on Intelligent Business and Edge-Computing Research (ISIBER)
abstract: Electroencephalography (EEG)-based emotion recognition presents unique challenges due to the non-stationary and complex nature of brain signals, which exhibit both spatial and temporal dependencies. This study proposes a hybrid architecture that integrates the Discrete Wavelet Transform (DWT), Convolutional Neural Network (CNN), and Mamba State Space Model (SSM) to enhance feature representation and classification accuracy. DWT decomposes EEG signals into multi-band time-frequency coefficients, capturing temporal and spectral characteristics essential for emotion analysis. The CNN module extracts spatial correlations between channels, while the Mamba module efficiently models long-term temporal dependencies using a state-space formulation. Experiments conducted on the SEED dataset demonstrated that the proposed CNN-Mamba model optimized with RMSprop achieved an accuracy of 81.64% and a Cohen's Kappa of 0.77, indicating substantial agreement in three-class emotion recognition. These results highlight the synergy between wavelet-based feature extraction and the Mamba sequential framework. Future work will explore attention-based mechanisms and multi-subject adaptation to further improve robustness and real-time performance.

title: Bayesian Active Learning with Polynomial Chaos Expanded Gaussian Process
authors: Dominik Polke, Elmar Ahle, Dirk Söffker
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer, Data Sciences and Applications (ACDSA)
abstract: The development of accurate surrogate models for complex physical systems and computer simulations often requires extensive and resource-intensive experimental efforts. In addition, the modeled physical dependencies exhibit complex behaviors characterized by non-linearities and non-stationary behaviors. To address these challenges, machine learning (ML) methods used to construct surrogate models should strategically minimize the number of queries and capture both non-linear and non-stationary behaviors. In this context, Polynomial Chaos Expanded Gaussian Process (PCEGP) is used as a method to model non-linearity and non-stationarity with limited data points. The Polynomial Chaos Expansion (PCE) is utilized in this approach to compute the hyperparameters of the Gaussian process (GP), enhancing the model's ability to capture complex dependencies between inputs and outputs. A key advantage of PCEGP is its simple model structure, which enables the flexible generation of nonstationary lengthscale parameters in the GP. Unlike established non-stationary GP approaches such as deep GP (DGP) or GP models with input partitioning, the introduced PCEGP method retains its interpretability while modeling complex non-stationary behavior. The PCEGP model is introduced as a fully Bayesian surrogate for active learning (AL), demonstrating low prediction errors across various non-stationary benchmarks. Its performance competes with or surpasses stationary GPs, DGPs, and inputpartitioned GP models.

title: PSO-Driven Optimization of Interleaved Landsman Converter for Efficient Hybrid Energy Management
authors: Venkatesh Kumar S, Vidhya A, Karthick M, Bharani V, Monisha S, Parthiban S
publish: 2025 International Conference on Emerging Technologies in Electronics and Green Energy (ICETEG)
abstract: This paper introduces a Particle-Swarm-Optimization (PSO) based Energy Management System (EMS) for a hybrid DC microgrid employing an Interleaved Landsman Converter (ILC). The EMS optimizes the duty cycles of converters in real-time to reduce DC bus voltage ripple and switching losses while maximizing harvestable renewable energy. MATLAB/Simulink simulations with typical irradiance and wind conditions demonstrate that, with respect to a PI controller that is finely tuned, the PSO-based controller enhances overall converter efficiency from 85.0 % to 92.0%, lowers DC bus voltage peak-to-peak ripple from 8.0 Vpp to 3.5 Vpp (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\approx$</tex> 56.3% reduction), shortens settling time for 50 % load step from 40 ms to 22 ms (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\approx$</tex> 45% improvement), and lowers battery SoC transient variance by 36.7 %. Major contributions are: (i) derivation of an averaged state-space model of the ILC and small-signal linearization; (ii) a PSO formulation that optimizes voltage deviation, ripple and switching loss simultaneously; and (iii) quantitative performance comparison (efficiency, ripple, THD, settling time, switching loss) demonstrating the superior performance of the proposed EMS in transient and steady-state.

title: Aggressive Behaviour Detection Using CNNs for Violence and Non-Violence Classification
authors: M. Marimuthu, D. Vidyabharathi, G. Mohanraj, P. Dhavakumar, V. Sathiyamoorthi, V. Sathiyapriya
publish: 2025 International Conference on Emerging Technologies in Electronics and Green Energy (ICETEG)
abstract: The increasing prevalence of online and offline violence, there is an urgent need for intelligent surveillance systems which automatically monitor violent behaviour in real time. In this study, a deep learning-based model based on Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) along with You Only Look Once (YOLO) algorithm is proposed for classifying a video-frame/image as violent or non-violent. YOLO algorithm:(You only look once) real-time object detection that identifies the aggressive actions in the sequences of video. Spatial and temporal feature extraction using CNNs can improve the performance of the model to classify violent and non-violent behaviours, respectively. In this work a large-scale set of images depicting violence is used to provide labelled dataset and the system is capable to detect detection with high accuracy and efficiency appropriate for real time surveillance system. The method includes, preprocessing of video frames, augmenting data followed by Feature extraction with CNNs and final detection using Yolo which runs it on real-time. In this work, we benchmark the proposed model against existing deep learning architectures, and we show its superiority in speed and accuracy. According to experimental results, the integration of YOLO with CNNs provides a solid backbone for the detection of aggressive behaviour in public spaces, social media content, and police prosecution contexts. This kind of research will expand public safety and give way to detecting and preventing violence automatically.

title: EyeQ: Smart Cloud Vision System for Automated Recognition and Analytics Using Machine Intelligence
authors: P. Arivubrakan, Manish Kumar S, Hari Krishnan M
publish: 2026 International Conference on Visual Analytics and Data Visualization (ICVADV)
abstract: The machine intelligence allowed machines to analyze, think, and act intelligently that producing the visual data with human-like accuracy in recent years. The automated video analysis is performed in AWS Rekognition Video, which is a cloud-based deep learning service, for detecting objects, visual faces and unsafe activities in real time. The machine intelligence integrates AWS services for video storage, the video recognition API for intelligent analysis and visualization dashboard for result interpretation. The framework aims to improve surveillance efficiency, encourage public safety and support media content moderation by providing accurate, timestamped metadata for every detected activity. The experimental evaluations show that the system achieves high precision in recognition tasks while significantly reducing the manual effort and analysis time. This implementation demonstrates the potential of cloud-based AI solutions in real-world video analytics applications with a high accuracy of 98%.

title: From Earth to Algorithm: Enhancing Soil Quality with AI
authors: Shrijan Paudel, Mansi Bhavsar, Rushit Dave
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer, Data Sciences and Applications (ACDSA)
abstract: Modern agriculture is facing challenges to maintain soil quality and crop production because of an unbalanced supply of fertilizer and nutrients to plants. This study presents the use of an Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Internet of Things (IoT) based frameworks for enhancing soil quality and crop recommendation. Using tools like soil sensors and spectroscopy-based systems, data on pH, moisture, and nutrient level (NPK) were collected and analyzed using machine learning models, including Random Forest Algorithm, Light Gradient Boosting Machine (LightGBM) and Artificial Neural Network (ANN). These models are trained and validated using various datasets from the research done in the Soil Research Centre, Pokhara, and public agricultural repositories such as Kaggle. Comparative evaluation between these models shows that the tree-based models, Random Forest and LightGBM, achieved high accuracy and are more reliable while requiring less computational power, making them practical for real-world farming applications. The proposed system enables real-time soil monitoring and recommendations of fertilizer and nutrients, which reduces the need for manual testing and supports to maintain sustainable environment. This research demonstrates the impact of AI and machine learning tools and techniques in maintaining soil and breaks the traditional concept of Farming.

title: Not Just Copper: Unveiling the Strong Performance of Steel Grounding Systems
authors: Shafar Zidan Nugraha, Moses Yohanes Darryl Hutapea, Ridwan Fauzan Dhoifullah, Burhanuddin Halimi
publish: 2025 5th International Symposium on Materials and Electrical Engineering (ISMEE)
abstract: This paper presents a comprehensive analysis of ground potential rise (GPR) phenomena in electrical power systems, emphasizing the performance comparison between steel and copper grounding electrodes under homogeneous soil conditions. Using Finite Element Method (FEM) based numerical simulations validated with ETAP software modeling, the study investigates various grounding configurations, including single-rod, isolated, and integrated systems to assess their effectiveness in mitigating potential gradients. The results demonstrate that integrated grounding systems significantly reduce ground potential rise, step, and touch voltages by creating equipotential regions that evenly distribute fault currents. Moreover, the influence of soil resistivity and grounding geometry was found to be more dominant than the intrinsic conductivity of electrode materials. Consequently, steel electrodes exhibit comparable electrical performance to copper, making them a cost-effective and vandalism-resistant alternative for grounding applications, particularly in Indonesia. The combined analytical and numerical evaluations confirm that integrated steel-based grounding systems offer the most practical and reliable solution for ensuring safety and stability in modern power networks.

title: Development of Internet of Things (IoT)-Based Electrical Parameter Monitoring System on Automatic Voltage Regulators Using Fuzzy Methods
authors: Jejen Suwaji, Dwi Prasetyo, Lukman Medriavin Silalahi, Irfan Kampono, Agus Dendi Rochendi, Abdul Hamid
publish: 2026 International Seminar on Intelligent Business and Edge-Computing Research (ISIBER)
abstract: The Automatic Voltage Regulator (AVR) functions to stabilize the electrical supply voltage. However, the AVR output monitoring system still uses an analog display. This study develops an AVR output electrical parameter monitoring system based on the Internet of Things (IoT), which is also equipped with real-time notification capabilities using a Fuzzy Logic approach. The system integrates a PZEM004T sensor supported by a NodeMCU ESP8266 as the microcontroller, which is capable of processing electrical parameter measurements and sending information that is then visualized through the Blynk cloud platform. The implementation of Fuzzy Logic can optimize the notification system in determining the priority level of alert urgency by analyzing several parameters simultaneously, namely voltage, current, and power. This research method is an experimental method that includes hardware and software design, implementation of an intelligent notification system based on Fuzzy Logic, and overall system performance testing. The test results show that the system successfully monitors voltage, current, and power parameters in real-time with an accuracy rate of 98%, and sends notifications based on the urgency level determined by the Fuzzy Logic system with a classification accuracy of 95%.

title: Physical Validation of Single Sensor Thermal Model Using Special Layout Analysis for Cooling Effectiveness Quantification
authors: Ze Cellier, Dong Yang, Weijun Wang, Haikuan Qiu
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer, Data Sciences and Applications (ACDSA)
abstract: The integration of high-performance computers (HPCs) into automotive architectures is becoming standard to support advanced functionalities such as ADAS and infotainment. These systems increasingly rely on high density system on chip (SoC) components, which pose significant thermal management challenges due to their compact form factors. In additional to advanced cooling hardware, thermal aware task scheduling offers a promising software-based strategy to enhance computational efficiency and mitigate thermal hotspots. This study lays the groundwork for thermal aware scheduling in automotive HPCs. Using an infotainment function oriented HPCs as study sample, we characterize the thermal behavior of SoC by executing diverse workloads, capturing data from 54 ondie temperature sensors and 40 telemetry metrics in real time. Both fixed workload tests and an AI workload stress test are executed under ambient condition with and without active cooling. Our analysis quantifies thermal dynamics reveals spatial correlations within the SoC architecture. A thermal bottleneck is identified through data analysis. The identified sensor is positioned in the CPU cluster with strongest coupling to CPU domain. It is proved to be an effective poxy for CPU thermal behavior. RC model is developed for the identified thermal bottle neck basing on experimental data. The impact of active cooling on thermal resistance is quantified, showing a reduction of 78 to 83 % in thermal resistance between the CPU cluster and its local sensor. The power dissipation is estimated basing on telemetry data, and derived parameters align with physical expectations, validating the model.

title: Learning When and How to Update Memory for Video Object Segmentation
authors: Shengye Qiao, Changqun Xia, Yanjie Liang, Xiaowu Chen, Jia Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence
abstract: Recent progress in semi-supervised video object segmentation has largely hinged on memory-based methods. However, when faced with increasingly tough challenges emerging in complex scenarios, such as fundamental semantic transformations and severe spatial deformations, the fixed-interval memory update mechanism usually adopted in these memory-based methods is insufficient to align with the pivotal moments of object changes. This inflexible mechanism motivates us to design an adaptive memory update mechanism in response to the semantic-spatial changes of target objects. To this end, we propose a novel Change-Sensitive Network (CSNet) to learn when and how to update memory to effectively address intricate challenges in complex scenarios. Specifically, wefirst design an Adaptive Perception-Capture module with a hierarchical contrastive learning loss to determine when to update memory moments by measuring the extent of object changes, thus dividing entire videos into different object-change clips. To further extract and highlight object changes to assist in the segmentation of frames after changes occur, we construct Dynamic Memory Update modules to redefine how to update memory by smoothly retaining the object prototypes within clips and dynamically amplifying the object variations across clips. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our proposed CSNet exhibits clear superiority when evaluated on eight datasets covering three kinds: common, complex and long-video datasets.

title: Endogenous Intelligence in Electro-Hydraulic Control Systems: A Flow-Based Framework for External-Sensorless Perception and Control
authors: He Liu, Xingyu Zhao, Lei Ge, Yunxiao Hao, Long Quan
publish: IEEE/ASME Transactions on Mechatronics
abstract: Industrial automation has achieved remarkable success in stable environments, but its technical paradigms are difficult to transplant to vast and unstructured field environments. At present, a fundamental contradiction exists between the reliability and intelligence of mobile machinery: relying on precise sensors makes systems extremely vulnerable under harsh working conditions, while pursuing reliability without external sensors renders machines unable to achieve autonomous operation or intelligence. To resolve this challenge, this article proposes a new paradigm of endogenous intelligence that circumvents this contradiction by mining and integrating inherent flow information within electrohydraulic systems. Unlike conventional soft sensing approaches that rely on data-driven estimation, our paradigm achieves hardware-level integration of flow perception and control through an endogenous intelligence electrohydraulic proportional valve. This valve incorporates an actively regulated flow sensor and a flow feedback mechanism within a single body, enabling high-precision flow measurement and closed-loop control without any external sensors. Experiments demonstrate that this system achieves high-precision autonomous control without external motion sensors, and exhibits unparalleled robustness under extreme working conditions where sensors typically fail. By utilizing internal information rather than external sensors, this article provides a fundamentally new technical pathway combining high performance and high reliability for mobile machinery autonomy.

title: WFCDCLIP: A CLIP-Based Framework for Weakly Supervised Farmland Change Detection
authors: Zhaoxiang Cao, Yuchun Huang, Longlong Ma, Yibo Zhou, Peng Zhou, Wenxuan Shi
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Farmland change detection is of significant importance for food security, land management, and precision agriculture. However, farmland scenarios generally exhibit high texture similarity and complex background noise, which not only makes manual annotation costly and easily disturbed but also complicates the automatic identification of fine-grained changes. Although weakly supervised methods based on image-level labels can significantly reduce annotation costs, they still face three major limitations under fine-grained changes and noisy environments: semantic mismatch between images and text descriptions, instability of pseudo labels due to noise, and insufficient depth of multimodal information fusion. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a CLIP-based weakly supervised farmland change detection framework, WFCDCLIP, whose core objective is to comprehensively enhance the modeling and discrimination of fine-grained changes through a multimodal semantic alignment mechanism. Specifically, based on the CLIP architecture, this study introduces an attribute-anchored averaged text prompt (AAATPrompt), which anchors text prompt embeddings to class-level change/no-change semantic prototypes and allows only residual adaptation to alleviate semantic mismatch between appearance variations and true semantic changes. A Monte Carlo sampling-based CAM improvement method (MC-CAM) is designed to suppress background interference through multiple samplings and weighted aggregation, thereby improving the stability of pseudo labels in noisy environments. Simultaneously, a text–vision bidirectional interaction mechanism (TVS) is incorporated at the decoder stage to deeply optimize the interaction between images and text, overcoming the insufficient fusion problem of existing methods under complex backgrounds. Finally, a local dense contrastive loss ( LLEA ) is proposed to enhance the discriminability of multimodal features in local regions, enabling better capture of fine-grained changes in farmland scenarios. These modules collectively form a multi-level multimodal alignment enhancement framework, which explicitly enforces semantic consistency between textual change concepts and fine-grained visual differences, thereby improving the reliability of weakly supervised farmland change detection. Experimental results show that our method significantly outperforms existing state-of-the-art approaches on the HI-CNA and CLCD datasets, especially in terms of Intersection over Union (IoU) and F1 score.

title: Architectural Evolution of a RAG System for Preliminary Blood Test Interpretation in Portuguese
authors: Anna Beatriz Da Silva Bittencourt, Samuel B. Mafra, Mateus Raimundo da Cruz
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer, Data Sciences and Applications (ACDSA)
abstract: Interpreting routine blood tests such as complete blood counts (CBCs) remains time-consuming and prone to variability among clinicians in Brazil. Large Language Models (LLMs) offer a promising approach to automating preliminary interpretations and supporting decision-making. We initially developed a Streamlit-based prototype that integrated BioBERTptall for semantic retrieval and BioMistral 7B for biomedical reasoning, utilizing a single-stage vector search with FAISS and relying on pre-defined fallback responses for robustness. Using reference cases from 101 Hemogramas and national guidelines, this initial system demonstrated significant limitations, generating clinically valid explanations in only 40% of the evaluated reference scenarios. The remaining cases required a fallback to curated references due to token limitations or linguistic inaccuracies, highlighting the limitations of a simplistic retrieval approach. To address these shortcomings, an evolved, more modular and precise system was developed. This advanced prototype introduces a two-stage retrieval pipeline, combining a vector search in ChromaDB with a cross-encoder for re-ranking, which demonstrates improved contextual relevance in a preliminary qualitative analysis. Furthermore, it incorporates superior analytical capabilities, with a dedicated prompt to analyze the complete clinical profile in search of patterns across multiple anomalous exams. Our preliminary findings highlight the importance of hybrid and sophisticated pipelines that combine biomedical expertise with advanced retrieval techniques to enhance the accessibility and reliability of automated CBC interpretation in multilingual clinical environments.

title: A Digital Twin Architecture for Cybernetic Event Processing for an Academic Project-Based Learning (PBL) Environment
authors: Reginaldo Arakaki, Leandro Augusto da Silva, Afonso Cesar Lelis Brandão
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer, Data Sciences and Applications (ACDSA)
abstract: PBL (Project Based Learning) academic environments involving stakeholders such as professors, students, systems, hardware labs and software and hardware developers, administrators, and other specialists profiles to get connected spaces to learn, are based on diversity of systems, data, and events, like students accessing materials, interacting withs project groups, observing and participanting in a professors instructions, doing tests and so on. These events have to be collected by a special class within the system —the Digital Twin —for processing and support of cybernetic capabilities. This paper positions and models a Digital Twin (DT, gêmeo digital) architecture for PBL environment observability. It maps stakeholders' concerns onto the five RM-ODP architectural views, specifies structural, behavioral, and process models that synchronize physical and virtual representations, and organizes dynamic scenarios that demonstrate how the DT can provide subsidies to stakeholders to evaluate overall learning results and efficiency.

title: Back Cover
authors: N/A
publish: 2025 18th International Convention on Rehabilitation Engineering and Assistive Technology (i-CREATe)

title: Shaking Stimulation Enhances Postural Stability in Older Adults with Comparative Effects of 5- and 10-Minute Protocols and Underlying Mechanisms
authors: Runhong Yao, Kouji Yamada, Masahiro Kudo, Fumio Okuyama, Yusuke Morita, Isamu Yanagisawa, Asaoka Yushi, Fumiya Inagaki, Takashi Kuremoto
publish: 2025 18th International Convention on Rehabilitation Engineering and Assistive Technology (i-CREATe)
abstract: Shaking stimulation has emerged as a novel nonpharmacological strategy for improving postural stability in older adults, primarily through activation of deep musculature and enhancement of proprioceptive feedback. Previous studies demonstrated limited effects after <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\boldsymbol{\leqslant} \mathbf{5}$</tex> minutes of stimulation, but the impact of extended protocols remains unclear. To compare the immediate effects of 5- and 10-minute shaking stimulation on postural sway under visual and non-visual conditions, and to explore possible neurophysiological mechanisms. Nineteen independent older adults <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(80.4 \pm 4.2$</tex> years) underwent platform-based shaking stimulation (8 Hz, 1 cm amplitude) for 5 or 10 minutes. Postural sway was assessed using a pressure sensor across six conditions: baseline (eyesopen), post-5 min (eyes-open/closed), post-10 min (eyesopen/closed). Parameters included sway area, trajectory length, anteroposterior (AP), and mediolateral (ML) displacements. Data were analyzed using paired t-tests. Ten-minute stimulation significantly reduced sway area (-21.5%) and trajectory length (-25.7%) under eyes-open conditions <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(\mathbf{p}<0.05$</tex>), whereas the 5- minute protocol showed no significant improvement. Under eyes-closed conditions, benefits of the 10-minute protocol were attenuated, with only trajectory length showing modest improvement <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(-16.9 \%, \quad \mathrm{p}=0.047)$</tex>. AP displacement paradoxically increased in eyes-closed conditions (+13.7%, p = 0.038). No significant ML effects were observed. Compared to 5-minute stimulation, a 10-minute protocol provides superior postural stabilization, particularly when visual feedback is available. These findings suggest that extended shaking enhances sensorimotor integration by strengthening somatosensory-visual synergy, whereas visual deprivation limits the efficacy. Shaking duration is a key determinant of therapeutic outcomes, supporting its application in fallprevention strategies for older adults.

title: An Integrated Framework for Pose Extraction and Tracking in Parent-Child Interaction Research
authors: Huu-Thiet Nguyen, Su Zhang, Isabella-Sole Bisio, Vishal Ramanathan, Angshuk Dutta, Thomas Allen, Amritha Varshini Devarajan, Cheryl Jing Yi Tan, Juan-Pablo Ortega, Domenico Campolo, Victoria Leong
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer, Data Sciences and Applications (ACDSA)
abstract: The study of parent-child social interactions has proven to be valuable in enhancing understanding of early child development and identifying avenues for intervention. Although rarely studied, analyses of the body pose patterns of parent-child dyads during naturalistic interactions (e.g. in videos) can yield valuable insights into their social dynamics and level of engagement. Resulting metrics such as interpersonal pose synchrony may inform our understanding of developing cognition and its disorders. However, existing methods for pose extraction and tracking lack user-friendly pipelines or fail to cater specifically to mother-child interactions, resulting in reduced effectiveness and accuracy of data extraction. This paper aims to address these limitations, by proposing an integrated methodology that offers a stable and reliable system for extracting and tracking body pose, specifically tailored for mother-child interaction videos. The proposed framework combines pose estimation and object detection techniques to extract skeletal representations of participants and track their identities throughout the video. This integration enables accurate assignment of individual identities to the corresponding skeletal data. To facilitate the adoption of this pipeline for automatic batch processing of videos, we provide the complete framework and detailed instructions in a publicly available GitHub repository. Researchers can leverage this resource to streamline their video analysis processes and extract valuable insights regarding parent-child interaction from their video data.

title: Leveraging Big Data Analytics for Childhood Obesity Research: The BIO-STREAMS European Digital Infrastructure
authors: Vasilis Apostolakos, Andreas Vezakis, Konstantinos Bromis, Thelma Androutsou, Stavros Pitoglou, Marios Prasinos, Philippos Sotiriades, Stavros T. Miloulis, Ioannis Kakkos, Marilena Tarousi, Ioannis Vezakis, Eleftheria Vellidou, Athanasios Athanasiou, Dimitrios Koutsouris, George K. Matsopoulos
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer, Data Sciences and Applications (ACDSA)
abstract: BIO-STREAMS demonstrates how large-scale, organized biobanks can revolutionize childhood obesity research while maintaining strict GDPR compliance. This paper presents a scalable infrastructure that successfully manages large amounts of sensitive health data, from over <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{5 3, 8 0 0}$</tex> participants across six European countries (42.400 retrospective participants from existing cohorts and 11,400 prospective participants from new clinical studies). The platform demonstrates its capabilities in handling high-volume, high-velocity data streams involving clinical biospecimens, digital biomarkers, behavioral patterns, and real-time mobile application data. The system processes large amounts of multi-modal health data, leveraging federated big data architecture, privacy-preserving analytics and AI-driven machine learning pipelines. The infrastructure supports real-time stream processing, automated data harmonization at scale, and synthetic data generation using machine learning models. Initial validation demonstrates the platform's capacity to handle unprecedented data volumes while enabling cross-border big data analytics for obesity research. BIO-STREAMS establishes a new paradigm for big data applications in pediatric healthcare, showcasing scalable solutions for managing sensitive health data at European scale. It also serves as a model for digital biobanks targeting complex chronic diseases requiring multidisciplinary research.

title: Data-Driven Evaluation of Seismic Fragility through ANN-Based PSDMs Trained on Structural Configuration Variables
authors: HakJong Chang, Junhee Kim, Sangjin Hahn, Teque Jeong
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer, Data Sciences and Applications (ACDSA)
abstract: Effective data-driven seismic evaluations for largescale building inventories rely on the streamlined definition of probabilistic correlations between ground motion measures and structural performance. Conventional approaches rely on extensive nonlinear time-history analyses to establish such relationships, which limit their applicability when large building inventories are considered. This study presents a data-driven framework for deriving seismic fragility curves of steel moment frames by replacing repeated nonlinear analyses with an datadriven probabilistic seismic demand model (PSDM). A comprehensive database of PSDM parameters was first constructed from nonlinear dynamic analyses of steel moment frame archetypes subjected to a broad set of ground motion records. Using this database, an artificial neural network model was trained to predict PSDM regression parameters directly from structural design attributes and fundamental dynamic properties, enabling continuous reconstruction of drift-intensity relationships without explicit nonlinear modeling. Fragility curves were subsequently derived based on the ANN-predicted seismic demand and evaluated against results obtained from conventional cloud-based methods. The proposed approach demonstrated strong predictive capability, with correlation coefficients exceeding 0.96, and produced fragility estimates that closely matched those derived from nonlinear dynamic analyses. By eliminating the need for repeated nonlinear simulations while preserving accuracy in damage-state prediction, the proposed ANN-based framework provides a practical and scalable alternative for seismic fragility evaluation of steel frame systems.

title: IoT-Based Water Metering System for Sustainability in Arid Regions
authors: María Z. Flores López, Jesús Hernández Cosío, David Schepers, Sarah Schwarzer
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer, Data Sciences and Applications (ACDSA)
abstract: Efficient water resource management in arid zones presents a challenging scenario for higher education institutions in Mexico. This situation underscores the urgent need for technological solutions to improve sustainable water management. This article presents the design, implementation, and validation of SMART UABCS, an intelligent water metering system based on the Internet of Things (IoT) concept, operating in public buildings. The system was designed as a pioneering alternative in Baja California Sur, one of Mexico's driest regions. Its manufacturing is affordable compared to commercial systems and devices. SMART UABCS integrates two custom hardware components and a web platform that allows for monitoring water consumption, detecting anomalies, and facilitating real-time decision-making. Implementation spanned ten months of successful operation, enabling the recording and identification of water consumption patterns. Among the results, it was observed that consumption followed a weekly cyclical pattern. Timely, real-time detection allowed for the repair of leaks and the identification of malfunctioning bathroom fixtures. Furthermore, the statistical analysis revealed significant relationships between consumption and environmental variables, which contributed to the improvement of the predictive models. This work provides evidence of a successful case study demonstrating the impact of IoT-based technologies in solving real-world problems arising from environmental challenges in geographical areas with high levels of water stress.

title: Ensemble Machine Learning for Peanut Yield Prediction from Weather Variables in Georgia (USA)
authors: Ramana M. Gosukonda, Chunhua Dong, Priyanka R. Kumar, Keith Howard, Hari P. Singh
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer, Data Sciences and Applications (ACDSA)
abstract: As agriculture embraces data-driven methods to enhance productivity, machine learning has become essential for forecasting crop yields. Weather data, with its nonlinear effects on crop growth, poses both challenges and opportunities for predictive modeling. This study applies six ensemble machine learning models, including Random Forest (RF), Gradient Boosting Machine (GBM), Extreme Gradient Boosting (XGBoost), LightGBM, Categorical Boosting (CatBoost), and AdaBoost, to predict peanut yield in Georgia, USA, using monthly weather variables. The goal was to compare model performance and identify key environmental and temporal factors influencing peanut yield variability. Model performance was assessed using statistical metrics including the correlation coefficient (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$r \pm$</tex> std), coefficient of determination (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{R}^{\mathbf{2}}$</tex>), mean absolute error (MAE), root mean square error (RMSE), Willmott's index of agreement (d1), and bias factor (BF). Results indicate that all ensemble models provided strong predictive skill, with boosting-based methods outperforming AdaBoost. CatBoost achieved the highest predictive accuracy on test data (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$r=0.89, R^{2}=0.79$</tex>), along with the lowest RMSE (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$424.2 \text{lb} /$</tex> acre) and strongest agreement (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{d}_{1}=0.94$</tex>), followed closely by LightGBM, RF, XGBoost, and GBM. AdaBoost consistently exhibited lower performance across all evaluation metrics. Feature importance analysis revealed that the summer growing season (June through September) encompasses the most influential months, reflecting the critical impact of environmental stability during the reproductive and pod-filling stages. Overall, the results demonstrate that advanced ensemble learning methods, particularly CatBoost and LightGBM, are highly effective for modeling nonlinear weather-yield relationships and provide valuable decision-support tools for weather-resilient, data-driven peanut production management in Georgia.

title: Design and Analysis of Geometrically Symmetrical Rectangular Patch Antenna with Circular Slots for Sub-6 Ghz 5G Nr (N79) Band and IEEE 802.11 AC Wi-Fi Applications: A Fractal Approach
authors: D.S. Bhargava, G. Seetharaman
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer, Data Sciences and Applications (ACDSA)
abstract: We propose a geometrically symmetrical antenna with circular slots for sub-6 GHz 5G NR (n79) band and IEEE 802.11 ac Wi-Fi applications in this research paper. The designed rectangular shaped symmetrical, fractal based planar patch antennas are fed by microstrip line feed. Two antennas with varied slotted patterns are designed using 1.6 and 3.2 mm substrate thickness and compared the performance concerning reflection coefficient, gain, radiation efficiency and directivity. The substrate material used is FR4 and the chosen design frequency is 4.5 GHz. The designed antennas provides a reflection coefficient of -32.068 dB, minimum gain of 3.3 dBi to a maximum of 4.22 dBi, maximum radiation efficiency and directivity of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{5 3 \%}$</tex> and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{7. 2 6} \mathbf{d B i}$</tex> respectively with a impedance matching of 50 ohms.

title: Robust and Secure Active IRS-aided Covert Multiuser MIMO Communications with a Multi-antenna Energy-harvesting Eavesdropper
authors: Wenhao Wang, Deli Qiao, Haifeng Qian
publish: IEEE Transactions on Communications
abstract: This paper investigates robust and secure active intelligent reflecting surface (IRS)-aided covert multi-user multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) communications in the existence of a multi-antenna energy-harvesting eavesdropper. In particular, an active IRS is deployed to establish a favorable wireless communication environment for improving both security and covertness of the system. We aim to maximize the total system secrecy rate by jointly optimizing the transmit beamforming and artificial noise matrices at the base station (BS) and the reflection-coefficients of the active IRS. Furthermore, we formulate the design as a non-convex optimization problem, explicitly taking into account energy harvesting capabilities of the eavesdropper, information security requirements of legitimate users, and covertness constraint against adversarial detection. To address the inherent non-convexity of the optimization design problem, we propose an effective iterative suboptimal algorithm. In particular, we first transform the original optimization problem into a tractable form by employing the weighted minimum mean-square error (WMMSE), general sign-definiteness, and S-Procedure methods. Subsequently, we exploit the block coordinate descent (BCD) approach to decompose the coupled optimization matrices. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed scheme can substantially outperform various baseline schemes adopting existing solutions. Besides, our results highlight the superiority of integrating active IRS into MIMO communication systems for secure and covert transmission.

title: Defending against Patch-Based and Texture-Based Adversarial Attacks with Spectral Decomposition
authors: Wei Zhang, Xinyu Chang, Xiao Li, Yiming Zhu, Xiaolin Hu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Information Forensics and Security
abstract: Adversarial examples present significant challenges to the security of Deep Neural Network (DNN) applications. Specifically, there are patch-based and texture-based attacks that are usually used to craft physical-world adversarial examples, posing real threats to security-critical applications such as person detection in surveillance and autonomous systems, because those attacks are physically realizable. Existing defense mechanisms face challenges in the adaptive attack setting, i.e., the attacks are specifically designed against them. In this paper, we propose Adversarial Spectrum Defense (ASD), a defense mechanism that leverages spectral decomposition via Discrete Wavelet Transform (DWT) to analyze adversarial patterns across multiple frequency scales. The multi-resolution and localization capability of DWT enables ASD to capture both high-frequency (fine-grained) and low-frequency (spatially pervasive) perturbations. By integrating this spectral analysis with the off-the-shelf Adversarial Training (AT) model, ASD provides a comprehensive defense strategy against both patch-based and texture-based adversarial attacks. Extensive experiments demonstrate that ASD+AT achieved state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance against various attacks, outperforming the APs of previous defense methods by 21.73%, in the face of strong adaptive adversaries specifically designed against ASD.

title: Understanding the Fundamental Design Decisions of Retrieval-Augmented Generation Systems
authors: Shengming Zhao, Yuchen Shao, Yuheng Huang, Jiayang Song, Zhijie Wang, Chengcheng Wan, Lei Ma
publish: ACM Transactions on Software Engineering and Methodology
abstract: None

title: Risk-Guided Car-Following and Lane-Changing Model for Safe Navigation of Autonomous Driving Vehicles
authors: Zhenqiang Ma, Hongjuan Zhang, Si Xu, Disheng Zhang, Bijun Li
publish: 2025 International Ubiquitous Positioning, Indoor Navigation and Location-Based Services Conference (UPINLBS)
abstract: Car-following model (CFM) and lane-changing model (LCM) in intelligent transportation system (ITS) are the most prevalent continuous maneuvers behaviors in traffic flow. Most existing studies model these two behaviors independently, leading to the potential oversight of the consistency between them. Therefore, this paper proposes a risk-guided unified modeling approach for CFM and LCM to realize a reliable and efficient navigation system. First, a dynamically varying driving risk field model is proposed on the basis of vehicles' physical properties and motion characteristics, in order to capture the variations of risk induced by the vehicles during in the traffic environment. Meanwhile, a road risk field model incorporating curvature factors is developed to characterize the risk constraints that typical road markings impose on driving behaviors. Then, an improved nonlinear model predictive control (NMPC) method with multi-objective optimization is presented to integrate the LCM and CFM models. The risk borne by the ego vehicle is represented by superimposing scalar risk values and vector risks respectively, thereby guiding the ego-vehicle to move in the direction of decreasing overall risk. And a lateral phase function is designed to achieve a smooth transition between LCM and CFM behaviors. Finally, comparative analyses with existing models are conducted using driving data to verify the effectiveness and correctness of our model. It provides a unified CFM and LCM behaviors framework for safety navigation services across various scenarios.
